example_name: PSD2 Reachability Non-Compliance by Non-Euro PSPs
example_background: Following the application of PSD2 on January 13, 2018, several payment service providers based in non-euro area Member States such as Poland, Sweden, and Romania faced challenges in achieving reachability for euro-denominated credit transfers. These providers needed to establish technical interfaces for SEPA transactions, including connectivity to payment schemes and adaptation of core banking systems. The regulation allowed up to 5 years for compliance from non-euro states, meaning until 2023, but some delayed beyond preparatory timelines, leading to incomplete implementation of APIs and message standards like ISO 20022. This resulted in limited payer reachability for cross-border euro payments, affecting customer access to seamless services across the EU Single Euro Payments Area (SEPA). National authorities monitored progress through reporting obligations, noting gaps in testing phases and certification processes during the transitional periods.
process_and_outcome: National competent authorities conducted assessments and issued compliance notices. Some PSPs received extensions or remediation plans, while others faced operational restrictions until full connectivity was achieved by 2023. No major fines were publicly reported, but ongoing supervision was mandated.
source_rule: (13) Given the technical requirements needed for reachability, it is, furthermore, important for a payment service provider of a payer to have sufficient time to prepare for compliance with the reachability obligation. Payment service providers should therefore enjoy a transitional period of a maximum of 1 year following the date of application of this Regulation in order to comply with that obligation. Since payment service providers from Member States outside the euro area would need to undertake more preparatory work, such payment service providers should be allowed to defer the application of the reachability obligation for a maximum of 5 years following the date of application of this Regulation. Payment service providers located in a Member State that has introduced the euro as its currency within 4 years of the date of application of this Regulation should, however, be required to comply with the reachability obligation within 1 year of the date on which the Member State concerned joined the euro area.
involved_parties: Polish Payment Service Providers (e.g., PKO Bank Polski)
relevant_rules: EBA Guidelines on SCA and Secure Communication

example_name: ING Bank's PSD2 Compliance Achievement
example_background: ING, operating across multiple EU Member States including the Netherlands (euro area), prepared for PSD2 reachability by investing in open banking platforms ahead of the 13-month transitional period post-January 2018. The bank upgraded its systems to support XS2A interfaces, enabling third-party providers to access account information and initiate payments. This preparation included API sandboxes for testing, certification under EBA standards, and bilateral agreements for reachability. ING ensured payer reachability for SEPA transactions within the 1-year window, facilitating seamless euro transfers for customers and TPPs.
process_and_outcome: ING successfully passed EBA and national authority audits by mid-2019, becoming one of the first major banks to fully implement PSD2 APIs. This positioned ING as a leader in open banking, with no enforcement actions required.

example_name: Santander's Early SEPA Reachability Compliance
example_background: Banco Santander, with operations in euro area countries like Spain and Germany, utilized the 1-year transitional period after PSD2's entry into force to fully enable reachability for euro credit transfers. The bank conducted extensive system migrations to support SEPA Instant Credit Transfer (SCT Inst) and standard SCT, ensuring 24/7 availability and integration with multiple payment schemes. Preparatory work included pilot programs with TPPs and compliance with reachability obligations under the revised Payment Services Regulation, allowing payers to initiate transactions via any compliant PSP.
process_and_outcome: Santander achieved certification and live operations within 9 months, earning recognition from the European Payments Council for timely compliance and contributing to SEPA harmonization.

example_name: Revolut's Delayed Non-Euro PSP Reachability
example_background: Revolut, originally licensed in Lithuania (euro area post-2015) but expanding from the UK pre-Brexit context, encountered hurdles in full reachability compliance during PSD2's initial phase. As it served non-euro currency users extensively, the firm required additional time for multi-currency SEPA integration and API development. Despite the 1-year rule for euro states, operational complexities led to phased rollouts, with some payer reachability features for euro transfers not fully available until 2020, testing the boundaries of transitional allowances.
process_and_outcome: Bank of Lithuania issued supervisory letters and required a compliance roadmap. Revolut completed implementation by 2021 without fines but under enhanced monitoring.

Payment service providers located in a Member State that has introduced the euro as its currency within 4 years of the date of application of this Regulation should, however, be required to comply with the reachability obligation within 1 year of the date on which the Member State concerned joined the euro area.

example_name: SEPA Migration End-Date Violations by Smaller PSPs
example_background: Under the SEPA Migration End-Date Regulation extended to PSD2 contexts, several smaller PSPs in euro area states like Italy and Portugal missed the 1-year preparation for full reachability post-2021 updates. These firms struggled with technical upgrades for ISO 20022 migration, impacting euro transfer reachability. The process involved insufficient testing of inter-PSP connectivity, leading to rejected transactions and customer complaints during the transitional phase.
process_and_outcome: Banca d'Italia and Banco de Portugal imposed administrative sanctions totaling €500,000 across cases and mandated corrective actions completed by Q2 2022.

example_name: Deutsche Bank's SEPA Reachability Leadership
example_background: Deutsche Bank proactively prepared for PSD2 reachability obligations within the 1-year transitional period, implementing robust XS2A APIs and SEPA scheme participation across Germany and other euro states. The bank conducted joint testing with over 200 TPPs and ensured payer reachability for all euro-denominated payments, including instant schemes, ahead of deadlines.
process_and_outcome: Certified compliant by BaFin in 2018, Deutsche Bank avoided issues and supported industry-wide adoption through developer portals.

example_name: N26's PSD2 Transitional Compliance
example_background: N26, a German neobank in the euro area, utilized the 1-year period post-PSD2 to integrate reachability features, developing dedicated APIs for account initiation and information services. Preparations included cloud-based infrastructure adaptations for SEPA connectivity, ensuring customers could reach any compliant payee PSP in the euro area.
process_and_outcome: N26 achieved full compliance by December 2018, passing BaFin inspections and launching TPP access without disruptions.

example_name: Swedish PSPs' Extended Reachability Preparation
example_background: Payment service providers in Sweden, a non-euro Member State, leveraged the 5-year transitional period under PSD2 for reachability obligations. Firms like Klarna and Swish operators invested in euro-SEPA gateways, but initial delays in 2018-2020 due to technical complexities in linking Swedish krona systems to euro schemes resulted in partial reachability until full rollout by 2023.
process_and_outcome: Finansinspektionen monitored via annual reports and required accelerated implementation plans; compliance achieved without penalties by deadline.

example_name: BBVA's Proactive PSD2 Reachability
example_background: BBVA in Spain prepared extensively during the 1-year transitional phase, launching one of Europe's first production-ready open banking APIs in 2018. This enabled full payer reachability for SEPA transactions, with support for PIS and AIS, and integration with multiple schemes.
process_and_outcome: Recognized by EBA as best practice; no regulatory interventions needed, facilitating broad TPP ecosystem growth.

example_name: UK FSCS Compensation for Wirecard Collapse
example_background: Wirecard, a German payments firm listed on the London Stock Exchange, collapsed in 2020 after revealing that €1.9 billion in cash balances in the Philippines did not exist, leading to insolvency. UK customers holding investment products through third-party providers faced significant losses. The Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS) processed claims from affected UK investors who had been sold products linked to Wirecard via authorised firms. This process involved verifying eligibility, assessing losses, and providing redress through the statutory compensation scheme designed for such scenarios involving failed financial firms.
process_and_outcome: FSCS declared Wirecard-related products eligible for compensation, paying out over £15 million to more than 1,000 UK customers by mid-2021. The scheme operated as an out-of-court redress mechanism, handling claims without litigation.
source_rule: (15) In order to ensure that redress is possible where this Regulation has been incorrectly applied, Member States should establish adequate and effective complaint and redress procedures for settling any dispute between the payment service user and the payment service provider. It is also important that competent authorities and out-of-court complaint and redress bodies are appointed either by designating existing bodies, where appropriate, or by establishing new bodies.

example_name: FCA Fine on Metro Bank for SCA Complaints Handling
example_background: Metro Bank, a UK challenger bank, between 2018 and 2021, failed to properly handle customer complaints related to Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) requirements under PSD2. Customers experienced issues with payment authentication, leading to declined transactions, and when complaining, the bank delayed responses, provided inadequate explanations, and did not uphold complaints in line with dispute resolution timelines. This affected thousands of payment service users seeking redress for service disruptions.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority investigated Metro Bank's complaints procedures, imposed a £10 million fine in 2023, and required the bank to pay £8.5 million in redress to affected customers plus £2.8 million in consumer credit redress.

relevant_rules: FCA DISP Rules on Complaints Handling

example_name: EBA PSD2 Complaints Statistics Report 2022
example_background: The European Banking Authority (EBA) collects annual data from national competent authorities on payment services complaints across EU Member States. In 2022, over 250,000 complaints were reported, primarily concerning unauthorised payments, execution delays, and refund disputes under PSD2. Member States' out-of-court bodies and designated dispute resolution schemes processed these, with variances in resolution rates highlighting the role of effective national redress mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: EBA published aggregated data showing 85% resolution rate overall, with recommendations for improvements in complaint handling by payment providers. No individual fines but guided supervisory actions.

example_name: Central Bank of Ireland Fine on PayPal for Complaints Delays
example_background: PayPal Payments (Europe) Ltd, operating in Ireland, from January 2019 to June 2021, did not adhere to statutory timelines for acknowledging and resolving customer complaints related to payment services, including disputes over unauthorised transactions and refunds. The delays affected numerous users, undermining timely redress availability as required for payment service providers.
process_and_outcome: The Central Bank of Ireland's Consumer Protection Division investigated, resulting in a €196,097 fine in 2023 after an enforcement process, with PayPal remediating internal procedures.

example_name: Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) PSD2 Payment Dispute Rulings
example_background: The UK Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), an independent out-of-court body, handles disputes between payment service users and providers. In cases post-2018 PSD2 implementation, FOS adjudicated on issues like liability for unauthorised payments via Apple Pay and app-based transactions, where providers sometimes refused refunds despite regulation stipulating user protections.
process_and_outcome: FOS upheld numerous complaints, ordering providers to refund full amounts plus interest in over 70% of PSD2-related payment cases in 2020-2022, demonstrating effective alternative dispute resolution.

example_name: BaFin Enforcement on N26 Payment Complaints
example_background: N26 Bank GmbH, a German digital bank, faced scrutiny for inadequate handling of customer complaints regarding payment execution failures and unauthorised transactions between 2019 and 2022. Users reported delays in refunds and poor communication, prompting regulatory intervention to ensure PSD2 redress obligations were met.
process_and_outcome: BaFin imposed administrative fines totaling €4.25 million in 2023 and mandated process improvements, including enhanced complaint tracking systems.

example_name: ACP R Complaint Resolution Portal Success
example_background: The Autoridade de Controle Prudencial e de Resolução dos Conflitos de Consumo Financeiro (ACP R) in Portugal established an online portal for payment service complaints following PSD2 transposition. Since 2019, it has resolved disputes involving refunds for non-executed payments and SCA issues, providing users direct access to out-of-court settlement without appointing new bodies.
process_and_outcome: By 2023, the portal handled over 50,000 complaints with 90% resolution rate within 20 working days, praised in EBA reports for efficiency.

example_name: FCA Action on Revolut Complaints Framework
example_background: Revolut Ltd, a UK-based neobank, was found deficient in its complaints management system for payment services from 2018-2020, including failure to provide timely final responses and inadequate record-keeping for disputes over international transfers and crypto-related payments under PSD2.
process_and_outcome: FCA's review led to a 2021 requirement for Revolut to overhaul procedures, with ongoing supervision; no direct fine but part of broader authorisation conditions.

example_name: Dutch AFM PSD2 Dispute Resolution Statistics
example_background: The Netherlands Authority for the Financial Markets (AFM) and the Dutch Financial Services Complaints Institute (Kifid) jointly manage payment complaints. Post-PSD2, they reported high volumes of cases on payment refusals and refunds, with designated bodies ensuring accessible redress procedures.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, Kifid resolved 92% of 12,000+ payment disputes in favour of consumers or providers as appropriate, within mandated timelines.

example_name: European Commission Interchange Fee Regulation 2015/751 Adoption
example_background: The European Commission proposed and the European Parliament and Council adopted Regulation (EU) 2015/751 on interchange fees for card-based payment transactions to address high multilateral interchange fees (MIFs) in the payments market. This regulation capped MIFs at 0.2% for credit transactions and 0.3% for debit transactions for transactions above certain thresholds, building on SEPA frameworks. It aimed to reduce costs for merchants, promote competition, and foster a single European payments market. The regulation included provisions for three-party schemes and exemptions, responding to market practices where high MIFs were set by card networks and banks, impacting direct debit and card payment efficiency. This complemented SEPA direct debit rules by providing a harmonized approach to interbank fees during transitions.
process_and_outcome: Regulation entered into force on 18 December 2015 with application from 9 December 2015 for debit and 9 December 2016 for credit. Industry adapted by adjusting fee structures, leading to reduced merchant costs estimated at €5-10 billion annually across the EU.
source_rule: (11) Currently, different business models are used for existing national direct debit schemes. To facilitate the launch of the SEPA direct debit scheme, it is necessary to put in place a common business model and provide greater legal clarity on multilateral interchange fees. For cross-border direct debits, this could be achieved, exceptionally, by establishing a maximum amount for the multilateral interchange fee per transaction during a transitional period. The parties to a multilateral agreement should, however, be free to determine a lower amount or agree a zero multilateral interchange fee. For national SEPA direct debits, the same national interchange fee or other agreed inter-bank remuneration between the payment service providers of the payee and of the payer could be used as that which existed before the date of application of this Regulation. Should such a national multilateral interchange fee or other agreed inter-bank remuneration be reduced or abolished during the transitional period, for example as a result of the application of competition law, the revised arrangements should apply to national SEPA direct debits during the transitional period. Where the direct debit transaction is subject to a bilateral agreement, however, the terms of such a bilateral agreement should take precedence over any multilateral interchange fee or other agreed inter-bank remuneration. Industry can make use of the legal certainty provided during the transitional period to develop and agree a common, long-term business model for the operation of the SEPA direct debit. At the end of the transitional period, a long-term solution for the SEPA direct debit business model should be in place in line with EC competition law and the Community regulatory framework. Within the framework of a sustained dialogue with the banking industry and on the basis of contributions made by the relevant market actors, the Commission intends to provide, as a matter of urgency, guidance as to the objective and measurable criteria for the compatibility of such multilateral inter-bank remuneration, which could include multilateral interchange fees, with EC competition law and the Community regulatory framework.

example_name: MasterCard MIF Cartel Fines 2017
example_background: MasterCard operated a multilateral interchange fee system where acquiring banks paid issuing banks a fixed percentage per transaction, maintained through bilateral agreements that effectively fixed MIF levels across the EEA. This practice persisted from the 1990s, preventing merchants from negotiating lower fees and distorting competition among payment service providers. The European Commission found that these fees were set at supra-competitive levels, unrelated to costs, and affected millions of transactions including those linked to SEPA schemes. The fees were upheld via network rules binding acquirers, impacting the transition to cost-based models as foreseen in SEPA direct debit harmonization.
process_and_outcome: After a formal investigation starting in 2007, the Commission imposed fines totaling €570.6 million on MasterCard in 2017, following a 2014 decision upheld by the General Court. MasterCard appealed but ultimately adjusted its fee structures to comply with caps.

example_name: Visa MIF Infringement Decision 2013
example_background: Visa’s UK MIFs were fixed at levels above competitive costs through intra-group agreements between Visa Inc. and Visa Europe, preventing downward pressure on fees. This affected cross-border and national transactions, conflicting with efforts to standardize SEPA direct debit business models and limit MIFs during transitional periods. Merchants bore high costs, reducing incentives for SEPA adoption. The fees were not justified by costs and foreclosed competition, as noted in the Commission’s analysis of payment schemes’ remuneration structures.
process_and_outcome: Commission prohibited the practice in 2013 (COMP/39.911), fining €435 million upheld by General Court in 2018. Visa implemented changes ahead of the 2015 cap regulation.

example_name: SEPA Direct Debit Core Rulebook Implementation by EPC
example_background: The European Payments Council (EPC) developed the SEPA Direct Debit (SDD) scheme, including the Core Rulebook, to standardize direct debit across EU member states. This involved agreeing on a common business model with zero MIF for core SDD transactions, facilitating migration from national schemes by 1 August 2014. National schemes transitioned to SEPA formats, with provisions allowing existing national interbank remunerations during a period, but prioritizing bilateral agreements. This ensured legal clarity and interoperability for over 30 countries.
process_and_outcome: Full migration achieved by 2016 end, with over 90% volume shift; EPC issued multiple rulebook versions (e.g., 2017-2019) incorporating feedback and ensuring compliance with EU regulations.

example_name: BNP Paribas Fortis SEPA Non-Compliance Fine 2015
example_background: BNP Paribas Fortis, a major Belgian bank, delayed full implementation of SEPA direct debit schemes, failing to adhere to mandatory migration deadlines and standardized formats for direct debits. This included issues with processing cross-border SDD transactions under the common business model and not fully transitioning from legacy national schemes, affecting payer and payee service providers. The bank’s systems did not fully support the rulebook requirements for refunds and exceptions during the transitional period.
process_and_outcome: National Bank of Belgium (NBB) investigated and imposed administrative fines totaling €1.5 million in 2015; bank remediated systems and achieved compliance.

example_name: Irish Banks SEPA Direct Debit Mandate Validation Failures
example_background: Several Irish banks, including AIB and Bank of Ireland, experienced systemic issues in validating SEPA direct debit mandates post-2014 migration, leading to rejected transactions and disputes. This stemmed from incomplete adoption of the common XML format and interbank remuneration processes under the SEPA business model. During the transitional period, discrepancies arose between national practices and SEPA requirements for multilateral agreements.
process_and_outcome: Central Bank of Ireland conducted reviews in 2015-2016, issuing reprimands and requiring corrective actions; no major fines but public enforcement actions led to industry-wide fixes.

example_name: German Banking Industry B2B Direct Debit Migration Success
example_background: German banks, coordinated by the German Banking Industry Committee, successfully migrated B2B direct debit volumes to SEPA B2B scheme by end-2016, adopting the zero-MIF model and common business practices. This involved updating multilateral agreements to align with SEPA rulebook, allowing national remunerations where applicable during transition, and prioritizing bilateral terms, ensuring smooth cross-border operations.
process_and_outcome: Achieved 100% migration ahead of deadline; Bundesbank monitored compliance, reporting high adherence rates and minimal disputes.

Within the framework of a sustained dialogue with the banking industry and on the basis of contributions made by the relevant market actors, the Commission intends to provide, as a matter of urgency, guidance as to the objective and measurable criteria for the compatibility of such multilateral inter-bank remuneration, which could include multilateral interchange fees, with EC competition law and the Community regulatory framework.

example_name: European Commission Guidance on MIF Compatibility 2009
example_background: Following SEPA direct debit recitals, the Commission engaged in dialogue with banking industry stakeholders to issue guidance on criteria for MIF compatibility with competition law. This included objective measures like cost-based justification, non-discrimination, and proportionality for interbank remuneration in direct debit schemes during transitional periods, aiding development of long-term business models.
process_and_outcome: Guidance published in 2009; informed industry schemes and subsequent regulations, reducing litigation risks.
source_rule: (11) Currently, different business models are used for existing national direct debit schemes. To facilitate the launch of the SEPA direct debit scheme, it is necessary to put in place a common business model and provide greater legal clarity on multilateral interchange fees. For cross-border direct debits, this could be achieved, exceptionally, by establishing a maximum amount for the multilateral interchange fee per transaction during a transitional period. The parties to a multilateral agreement should, however, be free to determine a lower amount or agree a zero multilateral interchange fee. For national SEPA direct debits, the same national interchange fee or other agreed inter-bank remuneration between the payment service providers of the payee and of the payer could be used as that which existed before the date of application of this Regulation. Should such a national multilateral interchange fee or other agreed inter-bank remuneration be reduced or abolished during the transitional period, for example as a result of the application of competition law, the revised arrangements should apply to national SEPA direct debits during the transitional period. Where the direct debit transaction is subject to a bilateral agreement, however, the terms of such a bilateral agreement should take precedence over any multilateral interchange fee or other agreed inter-bank remuneration. Industry can make use of the legal certainty provided during the transitional period to develop and agree a common, long-term business model for the operation of the SEPA direct debit. At the end of the transitional period, a long-term solution for the SEPA direct debit business model should be in place in line with EC competition law and the Community regulatory framework. Within the framework of a sustained dialogue with the banking industry and on the basis of contributions made by the relevant market actors, the Commission intends to provide, as a matter of urgency, guidance as to the objective and measurable criteria for the compatibility of such multilateral inter-bank remuneration, which could include multilateral interchange fees, with EC competition law and the Community regulatory framework.

example_name: UK Current Account Switch Service SEPA Alignment
example_background: The UK implemented the Current Account Switch Service (CASS) integrating SEPA direct debit portability, ensuring seamless switching of mandates and payments. Banks like Barclays and HSBC adopted common processes for interbank remuneration aligned with SEPA transitional rules, facilitating post-Brexit continuity.
process_and_outcome: Launched 2013, handled millions of switches with 99.8% success rate; Pay.UK ensured ongoing SEPA compliance.

example_name: European Commission Cross-Border ADR Platform Launch
example_background: The European Commission established the Online Dispute Resolution (ODR) platform in 2016 to facilitate the resolution of cross-border consumer disputes arising from online transactions across EU member states. The platform serves as a central hub where consumers and traders can submit complaints and connect with national ADR entities. It supports communication in multiple languages and provides guidance on applicable laws. This initiative addresses the challenges of fragmented dispute resolution mechanisms in the digital single market, where consumers often face difficulties in pursuing claims against sellers in other EU countries due to jurisdictional differences and lack of awareness of redress options. The platform has handled thousands of cases, enabling information exchange between ADR bodies and authorities to ensure consistent handling of disputes involving e-commerce, travel services, and financial products.
process_and_outcome: The platform became operational on 9 January 2016 following a transitional period for ADR entity registration. By 2023, it had processed over 85,000 complaints, with approximately 65% resolved amicably. Ongoing monitoring by the Commission ensures compliance and effectiveness, leading to improved cooperation protocols among member states.
source_rule: (16) It is essential to ensure that the competent authorities and out-of-court complaint and redress bodies, within the Community, actively cooperate for the smooth and timely resolution of cross-border disputes under this Regulation. It should be possible for such cooperation to take the form of an exchange of information regarding the law or legal practice in their jurisdictions, or a transfer or takeover of complaint and redress procedures if appropriate.
involved_parties: National ADR Entities (e.g., UK Financial Ombudsman Service)
relevant_rules: Regulation (EU) No 524/2013 on Online Dispute Resolution

example_name: ESMA Peer Review on Investor Complaint Handling
example_background: In 2022, the European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) conducted a peer review of how investment firms across EU member states handle client complaints, with a focus on cross-border cases. The review examined procedures for escalation, information sharing between national competent authorities (NCAs), and cooperation in resolving disputes involving retail investors. It identified variations in practices, such as delays in cross-border complaint transfers and insufficient exchange of legal practice information, particularly in cases spanning multiple jurisdictions like those between Germany and Spain. The review covered 31 jurisdictions and analyzed data from 2020-2021, highlighting the need for standardized cooperation to support the Capital Markets Union objectives.
process_and_outcome: ESMA published findings in July 2022, recommending enhanced bilateral cooperation agreements between NCAs and better use of existing IT tools for complaint data exchange. Several NCAs implemented improvements, resulting in reduced average resolution times for cross-border complaints by 20% in follow-up assessments.

example_name: Polish KNF Cooperation with Other NCAs on Cross-Border Complaints
example_background: The Polish Financial Supervision Authority (KNF) participated in a 2021 case involving a cross-border insurance dispute where a Polish policyholder filed a complaint against an insurer based in the Netherlands. The complaint related to mis-sold investment-linked insurance products. KNF coordinated with the Dutch Authority for the Financial Markets (AFM) to exchange information on local legal practices and facilitate the transfer of the complaint file. This cooperation was part of broader efforts under the European Banking Authority (EBA) and ESMA frameworks to streamline redress for consumers affected by similar products across borders. The case highlighted procedural differences in complaint assessment timelines and redress calculations between the two jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: The complaint was transferred to AFM, which resolved it in favor of the consumer with a compensation payout of €15,000. The process took 6 months, faster than average due to pre-existing bilateral protocols, and led to a joint report on best practices shared with other NCAs.

example_name: EBA Report on Cross-Border Consumer Protection Cooperation
example_background: The European Banking Authority (EBA) published a 2020 report on the supervisory practices of NCAs in handling cross-border consumer complaints in banking. It detailed instances where authorities exchanged information on unfair contract terms and payment protection insurance mis-selling affecting customers in multiple EU states, such as cases involving UK and Irish banks post-Brexit transition. The report emphasized the role of out-of-court bodies like ombudsmen in taking over complaints and the challenges posed by differing national redress schemes, drawing from data across 27 EU countries.
process_and_outcome: Recommendations were issued for mandatory annual cooperation meetings and standardized templates for complaint transfers. Implementation led to a 15% increase in successful cross-border resolutions tracked in 2022 follow-up.

example_name: BaFin and ACPR Joint Investigation on Cross-Border PSD2 Complaints
example_background: Germany's BaFin and France's ACPR collaborated in 2019-2020 on complaints from consumers regarding unauthorized payment transactions under PSD2, where German customers used French fintech apps. Over 500 complaints were exchanged, involving disputes over liability limits and refund timelines. The cooperation included sharing interpretations of PSD2 articles on strong customer authentication and dispute resolution paths, addressing gaps where national redress bodies had divergent procedures.
process_and_outcome: Joint supervisory action resulted in fines totaling €2.5 million on the fintech provider and standardized refund processes. A bilateral memorandum was signed for future cases, reducing resolution times from 12 to 4 months.

example_background: In 2018, the Central Bank of Ireland transferred a series of cross-border mortgage redress complaints involving Irish expatriates with UK-based lenders to the UK Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) post-regulatory handover discussions. The cases concerned tracker mortgage overcharges affecting around 200 customers. Information on Irish legal precedents was exchanged to align redress calculations, demonstrating practical application of cooperation mechanisms amid Brexit uncertainties.
process_and_outcome: FOS upheld 80% of complaints, awarding €10 million in redress. The transfer process was completed in 3 months, with a joint review published enhancing future protocols.

example_name: ESMA Breach on Insufficient Cross-Border Cooperation by Cyprus CySEC
example_background: In its 2022 peer review, ESMA noted deficiencies in Cyprus Securities and Exchange Commission (CySEC) practices for cooperating with other NCAs on cross-border investment complaints. CySEC delayed sharing information on Cypriot-based firms serving EU-wide clients, leading to prolonged disputes in cases like CFD mis-selling to Italian and German investors. This affected timely access to redress for retail clients.
process_and_outcome: ESMA issued formal recommendations and follow-up supervision. CySEC improved procedures, increasing cross-border complaint referrals by 40% within a year.

example_name: European Commission Infringement against Bulgaria on ADR Cooperation
example_background: In 2019, the European Commission initiated infringement proceedings against Bulgaria for failing to fully transpose ADR Directive requirements, specifically lacking effective cooperation mechanisms between competent authorities and redress bodies for cross-border disputes. This resulted in unresolved complaints from Bulgarian consumers against EU traders, with no established channels for information exchange or procedure transfers.
process_and_outcome: Bulgaria was referred to the CJEU in 2021. The country amended legislation in 2022, establishing a national ADR network linked to the ODR platform, resolving the infringement.

example_name: European Commission PSD2 Notification for Non-Euro Currencies
example_background: The Payment Services Directive 2 (PSD2) includes provisions allowing Member States outside the eurozone to extend regulatory protections to cross-border payments in their national currencies through a formal notification process to the European Commission. Several countries have utilized this mechanism to broaden the scope of payment services oversight. For instance, notifications have been submitted to cover payments in currencies such as the Swedish krona and Polish zloty, ensuring that consumers and businesses benefit from standardized rules on execution times, fees, and transparency for cross-border transactions. This process was designed to avoid repeated notifications for states that had already complied under prior frameworks like PSD1, maintaining continuity in regulatory application across the EU single market.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission reviewed and accepted notifications from multiple Member States, confirming the extension of PSD2 rules to national currencies without requiring resubmissions from prior compliers. This led to harmonized application across notified jurisdictions, enhancing market integration.
source_rule: (18) Extending the application of this Regulation to currencies other than the euro would have clear benefits, especially in terms of the number of payments covered. In order to allow Member States which do not have the euro as their currency to extend the application of this Regulation to cross-border payments denominated in their national currency, a notification procedure should therefore be established. It should, however, be ensured that Member States that have already complied with that notification procedure do not have to submit a new notification.
involved_parties: National competent authorities of non-euro Member States (e.g., Sweden, Poland)
relevant_rules: EU Regulation on cross-border payments (Recital 18 equivalent)

example_name: Sweden's Notification for Cross-Border Krona Payments
example_background: Sweden, as a non-euro EU Member State, notified the European Commission to extend the application of payment services regulations to cross-border payments denominated in Swedish kronor. This action aligned with the policy's intent to cover a larger volume of transactions by applying rules on maximum execution times and fee transparency originally designed for euro payments. The notification built on Sweden's prior compliance under earlier directives, avoiding redundant submissions. Swedish payment service providers, including major banks, adjusted their operations to meet these extended requirements for international transfers within the EU.
process_and_outcome: The Commission acknowledged the notification, enabling Swedish authorities to enforce the extended rules. No further notifications were required, and compliance was monitored through national supervisory mechanisms.

example_name: Poland Extends PSD2 to Zloty Cross-Border Payments
example_background: Poland submitted a notification to the European Commission to apply PSD2 protections to cross-border payments in Polish zloty, expanding coverage beyond euro-denominated transactions. This was part of Poland's efforts to integrate its payment market with the EU framework, benefiting consumers through guaranteed execution standards and information requirements. Leveraging previous notifications under PSD1, Poland ensured seamless continuity. The move addressed the high volume of cross-border payments in Eastern Europe, where national currencies dominate.
process_and_outcome: Notification accepted by the Commission in 2018, leading to enforcement by Polish regulators. Payment institutions implemented changes, with ongoing supervision confirming adherence.

example_name: UK FCA Non-Compliance with Cross-Border Payment Rules Pre-Brexit
example_background: Prior to Brexit, certain UK payment service providers failed to adhere to EU rules on cross-border payments in non-domestic currencies, including those extended via Member State notifications. Issues arose with prolonged execution times and undisclosed fees for sterling-denominated transfers to EU counterparts. Although the UK had aligned with PSD provisions, some firms did not fully implement transparency and speed requirements for notified national currencies, affecting millions of transactions.
process_and_outcome: FCA conducted investigations, issuing fines totaling over £10 million to multiple providers and mandating remediation programs. Post-Brexit, similar rules were adopted under UK law.

As a non-euro state with prior notifications, Denmark avoided resubmission. This ensured Danish consumers received equivalent protections for payments to other EU countries, supporting the single market's payment efficiency goals amid high cross-border trade volumes.
process_and_outcome: Commission confirmed the extension, with Danish authorities overseeing compliance through regular reporting from payment institutions.

Building on earlier compliance, the notification facilitated broader market access for Czech firms engaging in EU payments.

example_name: ECB Fine on OP Corporate Bank
example_background: OP Corporate Bank's payment processing systems exhibited persistent deficiencies in anti-money laundering controls from 2016 to 2018. The bank failed to perform adequate customer due diligence on high-risk clients and did not monitor transactions effectively for suspicious patterns. This included delays in filing suspicious activity reports and incomplete risk assessments for payment services involving third-country entities. The European Central Bank identified these issues during on-site inspections, noting that the bank's internal governance did not ensure timely compliance with supervisory expectations.
process_and_outcome: The ECB imposed a €3.27 million administrative fine in 2019 after supervisory review and required the bank to strengthen its compliance framework, including IT system upgrades.
source_rule: (14) Competent authorities should be empowered to fulfil their monitoring duties efficiently and to take all necessary measures to ensure that payment service providers comply with this Regulation.

example_name: EBA Guidelines Compliance by ING Netherlands
example_background: ING Netherlands proactively enhanced its payment monitoring capabilities in response to EBA guidelines on money laundering and terrorist financing. The bank invested in advanced transaction monitoring systems using machine learning to detect anomalies in real-time across its payment services. Regular internal audits and staff training programs ensured ongoing adherence to supervisory expectations, with transparent reporting to competent authorities on high-risk transactions.
process_and_outcome: Following EBA assessments, ING received recognition for its robust framework, avoiding penalties and serving as a model for peer institutions in supervisory dialogues.

example_name: ECB Action against ING Direct Bank
example_background: ING Direct Bank's anti-money laundering controls were deficient between 2012 and 2018, particularly in transaction monitoring and suspicious activity reporting for payment services. The bank experienced high alert volumes due to ineffective filtering, leading to delays in investigations and incomplete filings to authorities. Customer risk classifications were not updated timely, impacting payment processing oversight.
process_and_outcome: ECB levied a €8 million fine in 2018. ING committed to a multi-year remediation plan, including AI-driven monitoring tools.

example_name: Clearstream Luxembourg PSD2 Non-Compliance
example_background: Clearstream Banking Luxembourg failed to implement required safeguards under PSD2 for secure payment initiation services from 2018 to 2019. The firm did not adequately protect customer authentication data and lacked strong customer authentication measures for electronic payments, exposing services to unauthorized access risks.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg regulator CSSF issued a public notice and remedial orders. Clearstream upgraded its systems to full PSD2 compliance.

example_name: European Commission SEPA Progress Report 2010
example_background: The European Commission conducted an assessment of the Single Euro Payments Area (SEPA) implementation following the entry into force of the Payment Services Directive (PSD). The report examined the adoption of SEPA credit transfers and direct debits across EU member states, noting varying levels of migration from legacy national schemes to SEPA instruments. It highlighted challenges in cross-border payment processing, including the mandatory use of IBAN for SEPA transactions and the optional inclusion of BIC for routing. The report also evaluated direct debit usage, where SEPA Direct Debit schemes were rolled out with creditor identifiers and mandate management systems to replace national variants. Market developments showed increased straight-through processing but persistent issues with charge equality, where the EUR 50,000 ceiling for equal inter and intra-community charges was tested amid rising transaction volumes. The assessment included data from payment service providers on SEPA adoption rates, with some countries achieving over 90% migration while others lagged due to technical adaptations and customer inertia. Additionally, the report discussed the potential for removing settlement-based national reporting obligations as SEPA harmonization advanced, reducing the need for fragmented national statistics.
process_and_outcome: The Commission published the report to the European Parliament and Council, recommending continued monitoring and phased removal of legacy obligations. No fines were imposed, but guidance was issued to accelerate SEPA migration deadlines, leading to full enforcement by 2014.
source_rule: (19) It is desirable that the Commission present a report on the appropriateness of removing settlement-based national reporting obligations. It is also appropriate that the Commission present a report on the application of this Regulation, assessing, in particular, the use of the IBAN and the BIC for the facilitation of payments within the Community as well as market developments in relation to the application of the provisions on direct debit transactions. In the context of the development of SEPA, it is also desirable that such a report assess the appropriateness of the ceiling of EUR 50 000, which currently applies to the principle of equality of charges.

example_name: SEPA Migration Deadline Enforcement 2014
example_background: EU member states faced a hard deadline of 1 August 2014 to complete migration to SEPA credit transfers (SCT) and SEPA direct debits (SDD), as mandated under PSD2 preparatory actions. Many banks and payment service providers (PSPs) struggled with full compliance, particularly in ensuring all payments used IBAN and BIC where required for unambiguous routing within SEPA. National schemes like Germany's ELV direct debit were phased out, but some PSPs continued processing non-SEPA formats temporarily, leading to processing errors and customer complaints. The EUR 50,000 charge equality ceiling was scrutinized as transaction volumes exceeded expectations, with some cross-border payments incurring higher fees due to non-standard processing. Reports from the European Central Bank indicated that while 99% of credit transfers were SEPA-compliant by deadline, direct debit migration lagged in countries like Italy and Spain due to complex mandate conversions.
process_and_outcome: National regulators conducted audits and imposed remediation plans on non-compliant PSPs, with fines in several jurisdictions for delayed migration. The European Commission issued a retrospective report confirming overall success but noting persistent reporting burdens.
source_rule: (19) It is desirable that the Commission present a report on the appropriateness of removing settlement-based national reporting obligations. It is also appropriate that the Commission present a report on the application of this Regulation, assessing, in particular, the use of the IBAN and the BIC for the facilitation of payments within the Community as well as market developments in relation to the application of the provisions on direct debit transactions. In the context of the development of SEPA, it is also desirable that such a report assess the appropriateness of the ceiling of EUR 50 000, which currently applies to the principle of equality of charges.

example_name: Deutsche Bundesbank SEPA Direct Debit Assessment
example_background: In Germany, the rollout of SEPA Direct Debit (SDD) replaced national schemes such as ELV and BASIC direct debit. The Bundesbank monitored PSPs' adherence to IBAN-only mandates and BIC usage for international legs. Market data showed high adoption but issues with the EUR 50,000 ceiling, where larger corporate payments sometimes faced differentiated charges due to settlement differences. The assessment included statistics on refund rights (8-week window for consumers) and interbank settlement via TARGET2, evaluating if national reporting on settlements could be streamlined as SEPA volumes dominated.
process_and_outcome: Bundesbank published findings supporting reduced national reporting, with no major fines but requirements for PSPs to update systems. Contributed to Commission's broader SEPA report.
source_rule: (19) It is desirable that the Commission present a report on the appropriateness of removing settlement-based national reporting obligations. It is also appropriate that the Commission present a report on the application of this Regulation, assessing, in particular, the use of the IBAN and the BIC for the facilitation of payments within the Community as well as market developments in relation to the application of the provisions on direct debit transactions. In the context of the development of SEPA, it is also desirable that such a report assess the appropriateness of the ceiling of EUR 50 000, which currently applies to the principle of equality of charges.

example_name: Irish Central Bank PSD Enforcement on Charge Equality
example_background: An Irish PSP was investigated for applying higher charges to SEPA cross-border direct debits exceeding EUR 50,000 compared to domestic ones, contravening the equality principle. The case involved corporate clients using SDD for payroll and supplier payments, where IBAN/BIC processing was standard but fee structures differentiated based on settlement paths. This occurred amid SEPA maturation, with market reports showing increased SDD usage but fee transparency issues.
process_and_outcome: The Central Bank of Ireland fined the PSP EUR 100,000 following investigation and ordered fee adjustments and customer remediation.
source_rule: (19) It is desirable that the Commission present a report on the appropriateness of removing settlement-based national reporting obligations. It is also appropriate that the Commission present a report on the application of this Regulation, assessing, in particular, the use of the IBAN and the BIC for the facilitation of payments within the Community as well as market developments in relation to the application of the provisions on direct debit transactions. In the context of the development of SEPA, it is also desirable that such a report assess the appropriateness of the ceiling of EUR 50 000, which currently applies to the principle of equality of charges.

example_name: European Commission SEPA Report 2009
example_background: Prior to full SEPA rollout, the Commission assessed PSD implementation, focusing on IBAN/BIC adoption for euro payments. National reporting obligations for settlements persisted, with discussions on their obsolescence. Direct debit pilots showed varying success, and the EUR 50,000 ceiling was analyzed against emerging high-value payment needs in retail and corporate sectors. Data from EPC indicated 70% IBAN usage but low BIC inclusion rates initially.
process_and_outcome: Report recommended accelerated harmonization and informed Regulation 924/2009 refinements, leading to mandatory SEPA schemes.

example_name: French ACPR Fine on SEPA Non-Compliance
example_background: A French bank was found processing SEPA direct debits without fully implementing IBAN-only validation, leading to errors in cross-border mandates. This affected thousands of transactions, with some exceeding EUR 50,000 incurring unequal charges due to fallback to national BIC requirements. Market context included high SDD adoption in France post-2014.
process_and_outcome: Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel et de Résolution (ACPR) imposed a EUR 250,000 fine after audit and required system upgrades.

example_name: European Payments Council BIC Phase-Out Recommendation
example_background: As SEPA matured, the EPC recommended reducing BIC reliance for euro transfers where IBAN sufficed, aligning with market developments in routing. This addressed legacy national reporting tied to BIC-based settlements. Adoption rates reached 95% for SCT by 2018, with direct debits following, supporting evaluation of the EUR 50,000 ceiling's ongoing relevance.
process_and_outcome: EPC rulebooks updated, facilitating streamlined processing and informing Commission reports on obligation reductions.

example_name: Banco de España SEPA Charge Equality Investigation
example_background: Spanish PSPs were probed for charge disparities on high-value SEPA direct debits over EUR 50,000, where intra-Spain payments used cheaper national settlement while SEPA legs added fees. IBAN/BIC usage was compliant, but transparency lacked, amid 85% SDD migration.
process_and_outcome: Banco de España issued warnings and fines totaling EUR 500,000 to multiple entities, mandating uniform fee schedules.

example_name: ECB TARGET2-SEPA Integration Review
example_background: The ECB reviewed how SEPA instruments integrated with TARGET2 for settlement, assessing if national reporting could be minimized. High volumes of direct debits and transfers used IBAN/BIC, with data showing the EUR 50,000 ceiling rarely binding post-crisis recovery.
process_and_outcome: Review supported harmonized reporting, influencing Commission assessments without enforcement actions.

example_name: Payment Card Industry Data Security Breach at Heartland Payment Systems
example_background: Heartland Payment Systems, a major US payment processor handling millions of credit and debit card transactions annually, experienced a significant data breach in 2008-2009. Hackers inserted malware into the company's payment processing system, capturing data from approximately 130 million cards. The breach occurred prior to the full implementation timeline of the EU's Payment Services Directive (PSD), but it highlighted gaps in transparency and security standards for payment services that the PSD sought to address through clearer information requirements and obligations for providers. Heartland processed payments for numerous merchants across various sectors, and the incident exposed unencrypted track data transmission, inadequate monitoring, and insufficient customer notification protocols. The company had been operational since 1997, growing to handle over 100 million transactions monthly, but lacked robust segmentation and encryption practices at the time.
process_and_outcome: The breach was discovered in December 2008, leading to investigations by US authorities. Heartland cooperated with federal agencies, implemented remediation measures including system rebuilds, and paid fines totaling around $140 million in settlements with banks and card brands. No criminal charges were filed against executives, but the company enhanced its compliance framework and rebranded aspects of its operations.
source_rule: (20)For reasons of legal certainty and clarity, Regulation (EC) No 2560/2001 should be repealed.(21) In order to ensure legal coherence between this Regulation and Directive 2007/64/EC, in particular as regards the transparency of conditions and information requirements for payment services and as regards rights and obligations in relation to the provision and use of payment services, it is appropriate that this Regulation applies from 1 November 2009. It is appropriate to allow Member States to adopt measures introducing penalties for infringements of this Regulation until 1 June 2010.
relevant_rules: Payment Services Directive (PSD) 2007/64/EC (analogous transparency and security requirements)
PCI DSS (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard)

example_name: UK FCA Enforcement against Metro Bank for PSD Non-Compliance
example_background: Metro Bank, a UK challenger bank launched in 2010, faced scrutiny over its handling of payment services complaints and transparency in fee structures shortly after PSD took effect on 1 November 2009. Customers reported issues with unauthorized transactions, delays in refunds for failed payments, and unclear information on execution times and charges, which conflicted with PSD mandates for clear pre-contractual information and strong customer authentication processes. The bank had rapidly expanded its branch network and digital services, processing high volumes of retail payments, but internal systems failed to consistently provide required details on payment initiation, execution guarantees, and liability shifts for unauthorized payments.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) investigated following customer complaints and imposed a £10 million fine in 2021, alongside requirements for a redress program compensating affected customers up to £70 million. Metro Bank improved its systems and reporting mechanisms as part of the settlement.

It is appropriate to allow Member States to adopt measures introducing penalties for infringements of this Regulation until 1 June 2010.
relevant_rules: Payment Services Regulations 2009 (UK transposition of PSD)

example_name: European Central Bank Action against Wirecard AG
example_background: Wirecard AG, a German payment processing and financial services firm, collapsed in 2020 amid accounting fraud revelations, but prior investigations from 2015-2018 by the ECB and BaFin uncovered deficiencies in payment services transparency and compliance dating back to the PSD implementation period. The company provided payment processing for online merchants across Europe, handling card payments, e-wallets, and cross-border transfers, but issued misleading information on authorization procedures, refund rights, and data protection in customer communications. This occurred as PSD required explicit disclosure of conditions for payment execution and liability limits.
process_and_outcome: The ECB withdrew Wirecard's banking license in 2020 following insolvency, with fines and penalties exceeding €100 million from various regulators. Criminal proceedings resulted in executive convictions, and creditor recoveries are ongoing through liquidation.

It is appropriate to allow Member States to adopt measures introducing penalties for infringements of this Regulation until 1 June 2010.

example_name: Santander Consumer Finance PSD Fine by Spanish Regulator
example_background: In 2012, Santander Consumer Finance, a subsidiary of Banco Santander providing consumer credit and payment services in Spain, was found deficient in providing transparent information on payment service conditions, including execution times and charges, as required post-PSD entry into force. The firm managed installment payments and revolving credit linked to payment services for retail customers, but contractual documents lacked details on communication methods, consent requirements, and liability for non-executed payments, affecting thousands of contracts.
process_and_outcome: The Bank of Spain imposed a €9.3 million fine after an inspection, ordering corrections to documentation and customer notifications. Santander appealed but ultimately complied with enhancements to its disclosure processes.

example_name: ING Direct Italy PSD Transparency Violations
example_background: ING Direct, the Italian branch of the Dutch ING Group, was penalized in 2011 for shortcomings in pre-contractual information and statements of account for payment accounts, shortly after PSD applicability from 1 November 2009. The online bank offered current accounts and payment cards to Italian customers, but failed to provide standardized information on fees, interest rates, and payment execution details in a clear, comparable format, impacting customer understanding of rights and obligations.
process_and_outcome: Banca d'Italia fined ING Direct €1.77 million, requiring updates to all customer-facing materials and systems. The bank implemented compliant templates and avoided further penalties.

example_name: Deutsche Bank PSD2 Transition Fine by BaFin
example_background: Deutsche Bank faced enforcement in 2019 for delays in implementing PSD2 strong customer authentication (SCA) requirements, building on original PSD transparency rules. As a major EU bank handling vast payment volumes, it provided insufficient information on new authentication methods and liability shifts during the transition, affecting online payment services rolled out post-2009 PSD.
process_and_outcome: BaFin imposed a €3.125 million fine, mandating immediate system upgrades and customer remediation. The bank accelerated compliance and reported subsequent adherence.

example_name: BBVA PSD Compliance Success in Spain
example_background: BBVA, one of Spain's largest banks, proactively aligned its payment services with PSD requirements ahead of the 1 November 2009 deadline, implementing comprehensive transparency measures including detailed payment account terms, execution time guarantees, and clear liability disclosures. The bank updated its digital and branch channels to provide standardized information sheets, enabling customers to compare services and understand rights for refunds and unauthorized transactions.
process_and_outcome: Regulatory audits by the Bank of Spain confirmed full compliance, with no penalties issued. BBVA received recognition in industry reports for best practices, contributing to high customer satisfaction scores in payment services.

example_name: Revolut PSD Licensing and Compliance Model
example_background: Revolut, a UK-based fintech expanding into EU markets post-2015, obtained an EU payment institution license under PSD frameworks, demonstrating adherence through transparent fee structures, real-time transaction notifications, and clear terms on payment execution and refunds. From inception, it provided mandatory pre-contractual information and periodic statements compliant with PSD information requirements, serving millions of users across Europe.
process_and_outcome: Central Bank of Lithuania granted and maintained its license with clean audits. Revolut avoided enforcement actions and was praised for innovation within regulatory bounds.

example_name: BNP Paribas Fortis Belgian PSD Enforcement
example_background: In 2013, BNP Paribas Fortis, Belgium's largest bank by assets, was investigated for inadequate disclosure of payment service conditions, particularly regarding cross-border transfer fees and execution deadlines, post-PSD implementation. The bank handled extensive retail and corporate payment volumes but provided inconsistent information in account agreements and statements.
process_and_outcome: National Bank of Belgium fined €1.5 million and required process overhauls. The bank settled and enhanced its compliance training programs.

example_name: Crédit Agricole Cross-Border Payment Charges Fine
example_background: Crédit Agricole Corporate and Investment Bank was involved in practices related to cross-border payments within the European Economic Area. The bank applied fees for currency conversion and other charges on payments that exceeded those applied to corresponding national payments in the same currency. This occurred between 2008 and 2011, affecting numerous transactions processed through their systems for clients across multiple EU member states. The institution maintained that such fees were necessary for operational costs but regulators examined whether they aligned with the principle of equal charges for cross-border and domestic payments. Detailed records of transaction fees were reviewed, showing consistent application of higher rates for cross-border transfers compared to similar domestic ones in euros.
process_and_outcome: The European Central Bank conducted an on-site inspection starting in 2012, leading to a decision in 2014 imposing a fine of €5 million. The bank accepted the penalty without contesting the findings and implemented changes to its fee structures to align with regulatory expectations.
source_rule: HAVE ADOPTED THIS REGULATION:Article 1Subject matter and scope 1.   This Regulation lays down rules on cross-border payments within the Community, ensuring that charges for cross-border payments within the Community are the same as those for payments in the same currency within a Member State.
involved_parties: Crédit Agricole Corporate and Investment Bank

example_name: BNP Paribas Cross-Border Charges Enforcement
example_background: BNP Paribas was found to have charged fees for cross-border euro payments that were higher than those for equivalent domestic euro payments within France. This practice took place from 2009 to 2013 and involved retail and corporate clients executing transfers via SEPA systems. The bank's pricing policy differentiated based on the location of the payee's bank, applying additional costs justified internally as covering transmission expenses, though these exceeded national payment charges. Regulators analyzed thousands of transaction records to compare fee schedules.
process_and_outcome: Following an ECB investigation initiated in 2013, a €5.7 million fine was levied in 2014. BNP Paribas settled the matter and adjusted its payment processing systems to ensure uniform pricing.

example_name: Commerzbank Payment Fee Violation
example_background: Commerzbank applied charges to cross-border euro transfers within the EU that surpassed the fees for similar domestic payments in Germany. The issue spanned 2009 to 2012, impacting clients using the bank's online and branch services for SEPA transfers. Internal policies set distinct tariffs for cross-border operations, which were documented in fee schedules and applied systematically, even as SEPA harmonization progressed. Examination revealed that these fees included components not present in national transfers.
process_and_outcome: ECB inspection from 2012 resulted in a €2.45 million fine in 2014. The bank complied with the decision and revised its tariff structures accordingly.

example_name: Deutsche Bank Cross-Border Fee Penalty
example_background: Deutsche Bank imposed higher fees on euro-denominated cross-border payments compared to domestic payments within Germany from 2009 to 2012. This affected a wide range of clients, including SMEs and individuals, through various channels. The bank's fee model incorporated additional levies for international routing, which were applied regardless of SEPA integration, leading to discrepancies in pricing that regulators scrutinized through detailed transaction audits.
process_and_outcome: An ECB probe concluded with a €2.45 million sanction in 2014. Deutsche Bank accepted the findings and updated its compliance framework for payment charges.

example_name: Société Générale Payment Charges Fine
example_background: Société Générale charged fees for cross-border payments in euros within the EEA that exceeded those for national payments in France. This occurred between 2009 and 2013, involving credit transfers processed via SEPA. The bank's documentation showed tiered pricing where cross-border transactions incurred extra costs attributed to processing differences, prompting regulatory review of fee comparability.
process_and_outcome: ECB enforcement action in 2014 resulted in a €2.15 million fine. The institution implemented corrective measures to standardize charges.

example_name: ING Bank Fee Disparity Case
example_background: ING Bank N.V. applied charges to cross-border euro payments higher than domestic equivalents in the Netherlands from 2009 to 2011. Clients experienced this through retail banking services, with fees listed in public schedules that distinguished between national and cross-border SEPA transfers. Regulators conducted a comparative analysis confirming the pricing gap.
process_and_outcome: ECB decision in 2014 imposed a €225,000 penalty. ING adjusted its policies to meet regulatory standards.

example_name: SEPA Migration Compliance by Major Banks
example_background: Several major European banks, including those in the SEPA zone, transitioned their payment systems to fully implement uniform charges for cross-border and domestic euro payments by the 2014 deadline. This involved upgrading IT infrastructures, training staff, and publishing revised fee schedules that eliminated distinctions based on payment origin. Efforts included widespread communication to customers about the changes and participation in industry working groups to ensure smooth adoption across the single euro payments area.
process_and_outcome: Successful migration was verified by national regulators and the ECB, with no penalties issued and positive assessments in compliance reports. Banks received recognition for timely adherence.

example_name: ECB Cross-Border Payments Enforcement Summary 2014
example_background: In 2014, the ECB addressed multiple instances where supervised banks maintained fee structures for cross-border euro payments that did not match domestic rates. This followed inspections revealing persistent pricing differences despite regulatory requirements. Banks had fee grids explicitly higher for cross-border transfers, affecting millions of transactions annually across the eurozone.
process_and_outcome: ECB issued fines totaling over €20 million to nine institutions. All cases were settled, leading to widespread fee harmonization.

example_name: PSD2 National Payment Scope Dispute - UK Case
example_background: A UK-based payment service provider processed transactions where both the payer's and payee's PSPs were located in the United Kingdom, classifying them as national payments under PSD2. However, the provider applied cross-border strong customer authentication exemptions incorrectly, leading to questions on whether the transactions fully aligned with national payment processing requirements. The incident involved over 500,000 transactions between 2019 and 2021, with the PSP handling electronic transfers initiated by payers through mobile apps. Regulators examined the location verification processes of PSPs and the electronic processing methods used.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority conducted a review and issued a warning notice, requiring the PSP to update its compliance framework and reimburse affected customers for potential risks. No fine was imposed, but ongoing monitoring was mandated.
source_rule: 2. ‘national payment’ means an electronically processed payment transaction initiated by a payer, or by or through a payee, where the payer’s payment service provider and the payee’s payment service provider are located in the same Member State;

example_name: Banco Santander National Transaction Compliance
example_background: Banco Santander in Spain implemented a dedicated national payments platform for transactions where both payer and payee PSPs were within Spain. The system used electronic processing via SEPA schemes adapted for domestic flows, ensuring all initiations complied with location-based definitions. From 2018 onwards, this covered millions of monthly transfers, with automated checks confirming PSP locations through national registries.
process_and_outcome: Regulatory audit by the Bank of Spain confirmed full adherence, resulting in commendation and no corrective actions needed. The bank continued operations without interruption.
source_rule: 2. ‘national payment’ means an electronically processed payment transaction initiated by a payer, or by or through a payee, where the payer’s payment service provider and the payee’s payment service provider are located in the same Member State;

example_name: Revolut PSD2 National Payment Misclassification
example_background: Revolut, operating under a Lithuanian banking license but serving UK customers, processed payments between UK-located payers and payees. The company incorrectly treated some as national payments despite PSP headquarters being in Lithuania, affecting electronic transactions initiated via app from 2020 to 2022. This led to improper application of authentication rules for over 1 million transactions.
process_and_outcome: FCA investigation resulted in a £66,000 fine in 2023 for related PSD2 breaches, with requirements to enhance geolocation and PSP verification systems.
source_rule: 2. ‘national payment’ means an electronically processed payment transaction initiated by a payer, or by or through a payee, where the payer’s payment service provider and the payee’s payment service provider are located in the same Member State;

example_name: ING Netherlands Domestic Payment System Upgrade
example_background: ING Bank in the Netherlands developed an internal system for 'national payments' strictly limited to transactions where both PSPs were Dutch-based. Launched in 2019, it processed payer-initiated electronic transfers through iDEAL and other domestic schemes, with real-time validation of PSP locations via Dutch Payments Association data.
process_and_outcome: De Nederlandsche Bank audit in 2021 verified compliance, issuing a positive assessment and allowing expansion of the system.
source_rule: 2. ‘national payment’ means an electronically processed payment transaction initiated by a payer, or by or through a payee, where the payer’s payment service provider and the payee’s payment service provider are located in the same Member State;

example_name: N26 Bank Germany National Payment Fine
example_background: N26, a German fintech bank, handled electronic payments within Germany but failed to properly segregate national payments from SEPA-wide ones, misapplying the definition for PSP locations. Between 2019 and 2022, this affected payer-initiated transfers totaling €2.5 billion, with issues in payee PSP verification.
process_and_outcome: BaFin imposed a €4.25 million fine in 2023 for PSD2 non-compliance, mandating system overhauls and customer notifications.

example_name: BBVA Spain PSD2 Compliant National Platform
example_background: BBVA rolled out a national payments module in 2020 for Spanish domestic electronic transactions, ensuring payer and payee PSPs were both in Spain. The platform integrated with Bizum for P2P and payer-initiated business payments, processing over 10 million transactions annually with location checks against official registries.
process_and_outcome: Bank of Spain review confirmed alignment with PSD2 definitions, with no findings and recognition as a model implementation.

example_name: Monzo UK Domestic Payment Verification Failure
example_background: Monzo Bank processed UK-only electronic payments but lacked robust checks to confirm both payer and payee PSPs were UK-based, leading to occasional cross-border treatment errors from 2019-2021. This impacted app-initiated transactions for small businesses and consumers.
process_and_outcome: FCA thematic review led to a remediation program and £32 million in customer redress, with enhanced monitoring required.

example_name: Klarna Sweden National Payment Adherence
example_background: Klarna established protocols for Swedish national payments, limiting to electronic transactions with both PSPs in Sweden. Implemented in 2021 via their BNPL service, it included API integrations verifying PSP locations, handling payer-initiated 'pay later' options domestically.
process_and_outcome: Finansinspektionen inspection resulted in full compliance certification and permission for market expansion.

example_name: Starling Bank PSD2 National Scope Enforcement
example_background: Starling Bank in the UK categorized payments as national when both PSPs were UK entities but applied incorrect exemptions for low-value electronic transactions initiated by payees, from 2020-2023, covering retail and SME payments.
process_and_outcome: FCA fined the bank £28 million in 2023 for SCA failures linked to PSD2 scope issues, with an independent review ordered.
