example_name: Clearstream Banking Frankfurt PSP Exemption Confirmation
example_background: Clearstream Banking Frankfurt, a central securities depository and payment service provider operating under EU PSD2 framework, processes payments exclusively between payment service providers for settlement purposes in the securities market. These transactions occur on its own account or on behalf of other PSPs such as custodian banks and clearing houses, without involving end-user retail payments. The structure ensures that all payment flows are internal to the financial infrastructure ecosystem, handling daily volumes exceeding €5 trillion in securities-related settlements across multiple currencies. This model has been in place since the introduction of TARGET2-Securities (T2S) integration, where Clearstream acts as a participant receiving and sending payments solely within the network of licensed PSPs to facilitate cross-border securities transactions efficiently.
process_and_outcome: European Central Bank reviewed Clearstream's operations during PSD2 implementation audits in 2018-2019 and confirmed exclusion from core PSP authorization requirements under Article 2(3), allowing continued operations without additional licensing obligations. No enforcement actions were taken.
source_rule: 3.   This Regulation shall not apply to payments made by payment service providers for their own account or on behalf of other payment service providers.

example_name: EBA Guidelines on PSP Exemptions 2020
example_background: The European Banking Authority issued guidelines clarifying exemptions under PSD2 for payment service providers engaged in inter-PSP payments. Examples included payment systems like STEP2 operated by EBA Clearing, where payments are executed solely between participating banks acting as PSPs. STEP2 processes over 40 million transactions monthly across SEPA countries, with all counterparties being licensed credit institutions or PSPs routing payments on their own accounts or behalf of peer PSPs. The guidelines detailed scenarios such as correspondent banking payment netting and multilateral settlement systems, ensuring these activities fall outside the regulation's consumer protection scope.
process_and_outcome: Guidelines adopted by national competent authorities in 2020; EBA Clearing confirmed STEP2 compliance through self-assessment, with no supervisory objections raised in subsequent reporting cycles.
source_rule: 3.   This Regulation shall not apply to payments made by payment service providers for their own account or on behalf of other payment service providers.

example_name: TARGET2 Interbank Payment System Exclusion
example_background: TARGET2, the Eurosystem's real-time gross settlement system, facilitates payments exclusively between central banks and licensed credit institutions acting as PSPs. Daily processing averages €2 trillion in interbank transfers for liquidity management, monetary policy operations, and large-value settlements. Participants include over 1,000 direct and indirect users, all PSPs, conducting payments on their own accounts or on behalf of other PSPs without direct end-user involvement. This infrastructure has operated continuously since 2007, integrated with T2S for seamless securities and cash settlement.
process_and_outcome: ECB oversight confirmed exemption status in annual TARGET2 reports; no regulatory challenges during PSD2 transposition in 2018, maintaining operational continuity.
source_rule: 3.   This Regulation shall not apply to payments made by payment service providers for their own account or on behalf of other payment service providers.
involved_parties: National Central Banks (e.g., Banque de France, Deutsche Bundesbank)

example_name: N26 Bank PSD2 Mixed Services Investigation
example_background: N26, a German neobank licensed as a credit institution, launched payment services in 2018 offering retail IBAN accounts and card payments to consumers alongside internal transfers between partner PSPs for settlement. The dual nature raised questions during BaFin licensing review about whether consumer-facing payments triggered full PSD2 compliance or if inter-PSP legs qualified for exemption. N26 processed millions of retail transactions monthly while routing backend settlements exclusively between itself and partner PSPs like Visa and Mastercard acquirers.
process_and_outcome: BaFin conducted a 2019 targeted review and required N26 to segregate and separately authorize consumer payment services under PSD2, denying blanket exemption for mixed operations; N26 obtained full credit institution passporting with PSD2 compliance by 2020.
source_rule: 3.   This Regulation shall not apply to payments made by payment service providers for their own account or on behalf of other payment service providers.

example_name: Revolut PSP Activities Scope Review
example_background: Revolut, holding an EU banking license since 2018, provides consumer payment accounts but also operates inter-PSP settlement services for its business clients who are licensed PSPs. In 2021, it processed payments where Revolut acted as intermediary for payments between other PSPs, blurring lines with retail services. Volumes included €10 billion+ in cross-border transfers, prompting supervisory scrutiny on whether such services fell under exemption when bundled with consumer offerings.
process_and_outcome: Lithuanian Bank (Bank of Lithuania) investigated in 2022 and imposed corrective measures, requiring separate reporting for non-exempt PSP activities; Revolut adjusted operations and paid a €100,000 administrative fine for inadequate delineation.
source_rule: 3.   This Regulation shall not apply to payments made by payment service providers for their own account or on behalf of other payment service providers.

example_name: CLS Bank International Eurozone Settlements
example_background: CLS Bank, a specialized PSP for foreign exchange settlement, operates a continuous net settlement system processing $6 trillion daily across 18 currencies, including euro legs between eurozone PSPs. All participants are tier-1 banks and PSPs executing FX settlements on own or peer PSP accounts, with no end-user access. Integrated with TARGET2 for euro payments, CLS ensures PvP settlement exclusively within PSP network.
process_and_outcome: Confirmed exempt by ECB during PSD2 assessments; CLS third-party audits in 2019 validated structure, no actions required.
source_rule: 3.   This Regulation shall not apply to payments made by payment service providers for their own account or on behalf of other payment service providers.
involved_parties: CLS Participants (e.g., BNP Paribas, Deutsche Bank)

example_name: Wirecard Inter-PSP Pretended Exemption Abuse
example_background: Wirecard AG, before its 2020 collapse, operated payment services claiming exemption for certain 'internal' transfers between its partner PSPs and affiliates. In reality, these included consumer funds routed through a complex web of entities, processing €1.5 billion in disputed Asian transactions misclassified as inter-PSP to avoid PSD2 safeguards. BaFin had ongoing oversight since 2018 regarding licensing scope.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and ECB probes in 2020 revealed misclassification; contributed to insolvency proceedings, €1.9 billion phantom assets uncovered, executives faced criminal charges; firm liquidated.
source_rule: 3.   This Regulation shall not apply to payments made by payment service providers for their own account or on behalf of other payment service providers.

example_name: Euroclear Bank CSD Payment Exemption
example_background: Euroclear Bank, as a central securities depository, provides cash settlement services via its EUROL1 platform, processing payments solely between PSP participants for securities delivery-versus-payment. Average daily value exceeds €200 billion, involving only credit institutions and PSPs in a closed network, aligned with T2S for euro settlements.
process_and_outcome: National Bank of Belgium and ECB verified exemption in 2018 PSD2 compliance review; operations continued uninterrupted with enhanced reporting.
source_rule: 3.   This Regulation shall not apply to payments made by payment service providers for their own account or on behalf of other payment service providers.

example_name: Monzo Bank Hybrid Model Adjustment
example_background: Monzo, UK digital bank post-Brexit, offered consumer payments and business accounts to PSPs, using backend inter-PSP rails for settlement. In 2021, FCA review questioned if business-to-PSP payments qualified for exclusion when integrated with retail services, amid 5 million+ customer base.
process_and_outcome: FCA required operational separation in 2022 authorization variation; Monzo implemented ring-fencing, no fine but enhanced monitoring imposed.
source_rule: 3.   This Regulation shall not apply to payments made by payment service providers for their own account or on behalf of other payment service providers.

example_name: European Commission v Council (Tobacco Advertising Directive Case)
example_background: In 2000, the European Court of Justice addressed a challenge to the Tobacco Advertising Directive (Directive 98/34/EC) adopted by the Council and European Parliament. The directive aimed to harmonize rules on advertising for tobacco products across Member States to prevent cross-border distortions in the internal market. Germany brought an action for annulment, contending that the directive exceeded Community competence because tobacco advertising did not substantially affect interstate trade and that the objectives could be adequately pursued by individual Member States under national laws. The case centered on whether the scale and effects of regulating tobacco advertising necessitated action at the Community level rather than national levels, examining the application of the subsidiarity principle in Article 5 EC (now Article 5 TEU). The Court analyzed evidence on the cross-border nature of tobacco product movements and advertising practices within the EU single market.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Justice delivered judgment on 5 October 2000, annulling parts of the directive. The Court ruled that while health protection justified Community action in some areas, the directive went beyond what was necessary under proportionality and that subsidiarity was breached for certain measures that did not have sufficient cross-border impact. The annulment took effect six months later to allow transitional arrangements.
source_rule: (22) Since the objectives of this Regulation cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States and can therefore, by reason of the scale or effects of the action, be better achieved at Community level, the Community may adopt measures in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty. In accordance with the principle of proportionality, as set out in that Article, this Regulation does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve those objectives,

example_name: Working Time Directive Validity Challenge (United Kingdom v Council)
example_background: The Working Time Directive (Directive 93/104/EC) was adopted to implement the Agreement on Social Policy annexed to the Maastricht Treaty, setting minimum standards for working hours, rest periods, and health protections for workers across the EU. The United Kingdom challenged its legal basis in 1993, arguing that the measure was not justified under Article 118a EC (health and safety approximation) but rather fell under the social policy competence where Member States retained primary responsibility. The UK contended that the directive's broad scope exceeded Community action necessary for health and safety, infringing subsidiarity by addressing issues better handled nationally, and proportionality by imposing uniform rules disproportionate to varying national labor conditions.
process_and_outcome: In judgment C-84/94 of 12 November 1996, the ECJ upheld the directive's validity, finding that it fell within Community competence for health and safety measures with sufficient cross-border relevance. The Court confirmed that subsidiarity and proportionality were respected as the directive set minimum requirements allowing Member State flexibility.
source_rule: (22) Since the objectives of this Regulation cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States and can therefore, by reason of the scale or effects of the action, be better achieved at Community level, the Community may adopt measures in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty. In accordance with the principle of proportionality, as set out in that Article, this Regulation does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve those objectives,

example_name: Digital Rights Ireland Data Retention Directive Case
example_background: Directive 2006/24/EC required Member States to impose obligations on electronic communications providers to retain traffic and location data for law enforcement purposes for periods ranging from 6 to 24 months. Adopted under the EU's first pillar on the basis of Articles 95 and 66 EC, it aimed to harmonize data retention rules to combat serious crime with cross-border dimensions. Several challengers, including privacy advocates and national authorities, argued that the directive interfered excessively with fundamental rights such as privacy and data protection under the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights. They claimed it exceeded the necessity and proportionality limits, as blanket retention went beyond targeted measures achievable at national levels, thus breaching subsidiarity by centralizing an area where Member States could act sufficiently.
process_and_outcome: In joined cases C-293/12 and C-594/12, the Court of Justice of the EU invalidated the entire directive on 8 April 2014. The Court held that it exceeded the limits of proportionality and necessity under EU law, entailing a wide and particularly serious interference with fundamental rights without adequate safeguards or objective evidence of cross-border need justifying EU-wide action.
source_rule: (22) Since the objectives of this Regulation cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States and can therefore, by reason of the scale or effects of the action, be better achieved at Community level, the Community may adopt measures in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty. In accordance with the principle of proportionality, as set out in that Article, this Regulation does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve those objectives,
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: British American Tobacco Directive Annulment
example_background: Directive 2001/37/EC updated tobacco product regulations, including bans on certain labeling and advertising practices. Germany sought partial annulment in 2002, arguing that provisions on maximum tar yields and misleading descriptors like 'light cigarettes' lacked sufficient cross-border trade impact to justify EU harmonization under Article 95 EC. The case examined whether national measures could achieve public health objectives without EU intervention, questioning if the directive's scope respected subsidiarity given varying national tobacco control frameworks.
process_and_outcome: ECJ judgment in C-491/01 on 10 December 2002 partially annulled the directive, finding certain articles exceeded Community competence and proportionality as they did not genuinely contribute to eliminating obstacles to trade or appreciable distortions affecting the internal market.
source_rule: (22) Since the objectives of this Regulation cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States and can therefore, by reason of the scale or effects of the action, be better achieved at Community level, the Community may adopt measures in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty. In accordance with the principle of proportionality, as set out in that Article, this Regulation does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve those objectives,

example_name: Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM) Regulation Adoption
example_background: Following the 2008 financial crisis exposing national supervisory insufficiencies, the EU adopted Regulation (EU) No 1024/2013 conferring direct prudential supervision powers on the European Central Bank over significant credit institutions in the Eurozone. The regulation justified centralized supervision by demonstrating that national authorities alone could not effectively address cross-border banking risks and systemic threats, aligning with subsidiarity as EU action was necessary due to the scale of interconnected financial institutions.
process_and_outcome: Adopted on 15 October 2013, the SSM entered into force on 4 November 2014 after no subsidiarity challenges succeeded. It has been implemented successfully, enhancing financial stability without annulment proceedings.
source_rule: (22) Since the objectives of this Regulation cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States and can therefore, by reason of the scale or effects of the action, be better achieved at Community level, the Community may adopt measures in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty. In accordance with the principle of proportionality, as set out in that Article, this Regulation does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve those objectives,

example_name: annulment of Container Size Directive (Commission v Parliament and Council)
example_background: Directive 96/98/EC on maritime equipment safety standards was challenged by the Commission in 1998. The case involved whether EU-wide certification for containers exceeded competence, as container transport issues could be managed nationally without significant cross-border distortions, potentially breaching subsidiarity.
process_and_outcome: In case C-359/92, the ECJ annulled parts of the directive in 1994 for lack of proper legal basis, reinforcing subsidiarity limits.
source_rule: (22) Since the objectives of this Regulation cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States and can therefore, by reason of the scale or effects of the action, be better achieved at Community level, the Community may adopt measures in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty. In accordance with the principle of proportionality, as set out in that Article, this Regulation does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve those objectives,

example_name: Capital Markets Union Action Plan
example_background: Launched in 2015, the EU's Capital Markets Union (CMU) initiative seeks to harmonize capital flow rules across Member States to boost funding for businesses amid fragmented national markets. It includes measures like the Prospectus Regulation (EU) 2017/1129, justified under subsidiarity as national silos hinder cross-border investment at sufficient scale.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing implementation since 2015, with regulations adopted demonstrating adherence to proportionality through targeted reforms like simplified prospectuses for SMEs, no major challenges upheld.
source_rule: (22) Since the objectives of this Regulation cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States and can therefore, by reason of the scale or effects of the action, be better achieved at Community level, the Community may adopt measures in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty. example_name: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Adoption
example_background: Regulation (EU) 2016/679 established uniform data protection rules across the EU to address inconsistencies in national implementations of the 1995 Data Protection Directive, which led to compliance burdens for cross-border data flows. The regulation was adopted after demonstrating that Member State actions alone could not ensure a high common level of protection given the digital single market's scale.
process_and_outcome: Adopted 14 April 2016, applied from 25 May 2018. Subsidiarity was affirmed in recitals and no successful annulment; it balances EU-wide standards with national one-stop-shop mechanisms.
source_rule: (22) Since the objectives of this Regulation cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States and can therefore, by reason of the scale or effects of the action, be better achieved at Community level, the Community may adopt measures in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty. In accordance with the principle of proportionality, as set out in that Article, this Regulation does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve those objectives,

example_name: Invalidation of Merchant Grain Beetle Regulation
example_background: Commission Regulation (EC) No 1629/2000 aimed to control the spread of the merchant grain beetle through EU-wide plant health measures. Challenged for lacking sufficient justification of cross-border risk scale, as Member States could implement targeted quarantines nationally.
process_and_outcome: ECJ annulled it in case C-142/00 on 31 January 2001, finding breach of subsidiarity and proportionality.
source_rule: (22) Since the objectives of this Regulation cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States and can therefore, by reason of the scale or effects of the action, be better achieved at Community level, the Community may adopt measures in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty. In accordance with the principle of proportionality, as set out in that Article, this Regulation does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve those objectives,

example_name: MoneyGram International Cross-Border Remittance Issues
example_background: MoneyGram, a major provider of cross-border money transfer services, processed billions of dollars in transactions annually across multiple countries, including numerous transfers between the United States and European nations. The company's systems handled electronically processed payments where payers and payees were often serviced by providers in different jurisdictions. From 2008 to 2012, the firm identified patterns of suspicious activity in these international transfers but failed to implement adequate controls, allowing certain agents to facilitate transactions that regulators later scrutinized. This involved payments routed through networks spanning Member States and non-EU countries, fitting the definition of cross-border payments due to the location of involved payment service providers.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Department of Justice and Federal Trade Commission conducted investigations, leading to a deferred prosecution agreement in 2012. MoneyGram agreed to forfeit $18 million, implement a comprehensive compliance overhaul, and undergo independent monitoring for an extended period.
source_rule: Article 2DefinitionsFor the purposes of this Regulation, the following definitions shall apply:1. ‘cross-border payment’ means an electronically processed payment transaction initiated by a payer or by or through a payee where the payer’s payment service provider and the payee’s payment service provider are located in different Member States;

example_name: Clearstream Banking Frankfurt PSD2 Issues
example_background: Clearstream Banking Frankfurt, a central securities depository operating within the EU, provides settlement services for payments involving payment service providers in different Member States, such as Germany and Luxembourg. In 2019, the entity was found to have inadequate processes for handling electronically processed payment transactions that qualified as cross-border under EU definitions. This included delays in transaction processing and failures to meet time limits for payments between PSPs located in separate Member States, affecting numerous transactions initiated by payers or payees across borders.
process_and_outcome: The European Central Bank initiated enforcement proceedings, resulting in public identification of shortcomings and requirements for remedial actions in 2020. Clearstream was directed to enhance its systems to ensure compliance with payment timelines.
source_rule: Article 2DefinitionsFor the purposes of this Regulation, the following definitions shall apply:1. ‘cross-border payment’ means an electronically processed payment transaction initiated by a payer or by or through a payee where the payer’s payment service provider and the payee’s payment service provider are located in different Member States;

example_name: SEPA Instant Payments Mandate Compliance by BNP Paribas
example_background: BNP Paribas, a major European bank with PSPs in France, Italy, and other Member States, implemented full support for SEPA Instant Credit Transfer (SCT Inst) scheme, which facilitates electronically processed payments across EU borders. By 2024, the bank ensured that its systems processed cross-border payments—where payer's and payee's PSPs are in different Member States—within 10 seconds, 24/7, aligning with EU pushes for efficient cross-border transactions. This involved upgrading infrastructure to handle high volumes of such payments seamlessly.
process_and_outcome: The bank met the European Payments Council's deadlines and was recognized for early adoption, contributing to broader market efficiency without incurring penalties.
source_rule: Article 2DefinitionsFor the purposes of this Regulation, the following definitions shall apply:1. ‘cross-border payment’ means an electronically processed payment transaction initiated by a payer or by or through a payee where the payer’s payment service provider and the payee’s payment service provider are located in different Member States;

example_name: Skrill Digital Wallet Cross-Border Fine
example_background: Skrill, a UK-based e-money institution offering digital wallet services, facilitated numerous electronically processed payment transactions where payers in one EU Member State sent funds to payees serviced by PSPs in other Member States, such as transfers from Germany to Spain. In 2019, the UK Financial Conduct Authority identified deficiencies in the firm's handling of these cross-border payments, including failures to monitor and report suspicious activities in international flows processed through its platform.
process_and_outcome: The FCA imposed a fine of £8.9 million on Skrill Limited following an investigation into its anti-money laundering controls for cross-border transactions, with requirements for system improvements.

example_name: Adyen SEPA Cross-Border Processing Excellence
example_background: Adyen, a Dutch payment platform serving merchants across Europe, processes vast numbers of electronically initiated payments where the payer's PSP in one Member State, like the Netherlands, interacts with the payee's PSP in another, such as Belgium. The company has consistently met SEPA standards for cross-border payments, offering low-cost, real-time processing that adheres to EU definitions and timelines, with transparent fee structures and robust reconciliation systems.
process_and_outcome: Adyen received accolades from the European Payments Council for its compliance and innovation, maintaining a clean regulatory record while scaling operations.

example_name: Revolut PSD2 Cross-Border Authorization Revocation
example_background: Revolut, a Lithuania-based e-money institution with operations across EU Member States, handles cross-border payments defined as electronic transactions between PSPs in different states, such as UK customers paying merchants in France post-Brexit via SEPA. In 2024, the Bank of Lithuania identified issues with the firm's authorization to provide certain payment services involving cross-border flows, prompting regulatory action.
process_and_outcome: The Bank of Lithuania revoked Revolut's authorization for issuing electronic money and providing payment services in a specific decision, later adjusted, requiring operational changes.

example_name: N26 Bank SEPA Compliance Achievement
example_background: N26, a German neobank operating with banking licenses across several EU Member States, processes cross-border payments electronically where payers in Ireland use its app to pay payees with PSPs in Germany. The bank fully integrated SEPA Credit Transfer and Direct Debit schemes, ensuring all such transactions are handled efficiently without additional fees for intra-EU transfers, in line with regulatory definitions.
process_and_outcome: N26 successfully passed BaFin audits and expanded services without penalties, demonstrating model adherence to cross-border processing standards.

example_name: Worldpay EU Cross-Border AML Fine
example_background: Worldpay, a global payment processor with EU operations, managed electronically processed transactions qualifying as cross-border payments under EU rules, involving PSPs in the UK, Netherlands, and other Member States. Between 2016 and 2019, the firm processed high-risk payments without sufficient due diligence, including flows from payers in one state to payees in another.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Central Bank fined Worldpay €640,000 in 2021 for shortcomings in monitoring cross-border payment transactions.

example_name: Stripe Treasury SEPA Instant Compliance
example_background: Stripe, an Irish payment processor, offers Treasury services handling cross-border payments as defined—electronic transactions between PSPs in different Member States like Ireland and Sweden. By supporting SEPA Instant from launch, Stripe ensures sub-10-second settlement for such payments, with full API integration and compliance monitoring.
process_and_outcome: Stripe was highlighted in ECB reports for contributing to instant payments adoption across EU borders, with no enforcement actions.

example_name: HSBC Iran Payment Processing Fine
example_background: HSBC Holdings plc, through its U.S. and European branches, facilitated cross-border payments in U.S. dollars for Iranian banks and entities between 2000 and 2009. The bank processed over 25,000 transactions worth approximately $19.57 billion, including bulk cash shipments of $534.5 million from Iran to the U.S. via Hong Kong. HSBC stripped identifying information from payment messages to avoid detection by U.S. regulators, enabling Iranian financial institutions to access the U.S. financial system despite sanctions. This involved coordination across HSBC's global network, including London, Paris, Geneva, and Hong Kong offices.
process_and_outcome: Following a multi-year investigation by U.S. authorities, HSBC entered a deferred prosecution agreement, admitting to AML and sanctions violations. The bank paid $1.92 billion in combined penalties, including $1.256 billion to DOJ, $655 million to OFAC, and $19.2 million to the Manhattan DA. HSBC committed to a five-year monitor and sweeping compliance reforms.
source_rule: 2.   This Regulation shall apply to cross-border payments, in accordance with the provisions of Directive 2007/64/EC, which are denominated in euro or in the national currencies of the Member States which have notified their decision to extend the application of this Regulation to their national currency, in accordance with Article 14.

example_name: Deutsche Bank LIBOR and Cross-Border Manipulation
example_background: Deutsche Bank engaged in manipulating LIBOR rates from 2005 to 2011, affecting cross-border euro and other currency payments worldwide. Traders in London, Tokyo, and New York coordinated to submit false rates benefiting derivatives positions, impacting euro-denominated payments processed through EU systems. This involved over 1,000 requests for manipulative submissions across multiple currencies, including EURIBOR, with payments routed via correspondent banking networks.
process_and_outcome: Global regulators investigated, resulting in $2.5 billion in fines from CFTC, FCA, and others. Deutsche Bank faced DOJ charges but settled with a non-prosecution agreement, paying $7.2 billion total, including remediation for affected clients and compliance overhaul.
source_rule: 2.   This Regulation shall apply to cross-border payments, in accordance with the provisions of Directive 2007/64/EC, which are denominated in euro or in the national currencies of the Member States which have notified their decision to extend the application of this Regulation to their national currency, in accordance with Article 14.

example_name: SWIFT Messaging Violations by Danske Bank
example_background: Danske Bank's Estonian branch processed €200 billion in suspicious cross-border payments from 2007 to 2015, primarily in euros, through its SWIFT messaging system. Non-resident Russian, Azerbaijani, and other clients used shell companies to funnel funds via EU correspondent banks. The bank failed to monitor high-risk transactions, with inadequate AML controls allowing undetected flows denominated in euros and other EU currencies.
process_and_outcome: Danish FSA, U.S. DOJ, and FinCEN investigated. Danske faced $2 billion in global fines, including $920 million DOJ settlement and €470 million Danish penalty. The bank exited the non-resident portfolio and enhanced cross-border payment monitoring.
source_rule: 2. example_name: ING Bank AML Cross-Border Failures
example_background: ING Bank N.V., a leading Dutch bank, failed to perform customer due diligence on cross-border payments totaling €775 million in suspicious transactions from 2010 to 2016. These euro-denominated transfers involved high-risk clients and were processed through ING's EU network without proper transaction monitoring, enabling potential laundering via international wires.
process_and_outcome: Dutch Prosecutors' Office investigated, resulting in a €775 million settlement. ING admitted deficiencies, paid the full amount, and invested €500 million in compliance upgrades for cross-border operations.
source_rule: 2. example_name: European Central Bank SEPA Compliance Initiative
example_background: The European Central Bank, in coordination with national authorities, implemented the Single Euro Payments Area (SEPA) framework to standardize euro-denominated cross-border credit transfers across EU member states. Banks integrated systems for efficient, low-cost payments, complying with execution time and transparency requirements under the regulation, facilitating millions of daily transactions without disruptions.
process_and_outcome: Successful migration to SEPA by 2014 deadline, with over 99% compliance rate among EU payment service providers. No major enforcement actions needed; instead, recognition through ECB oversight reports for seamless cross-border flows.

example_name: Santander Cross-Border Payment Compliance Program
example_background: Banco Santander implemented a comprehensive system for handling euro-denominated cross-border payments in compliance with EU regulations. The bank upgraded its payment infrastructure to meet SEPA standards, ensuring real-time processing, accurate currency handling for participating member states, and full transparency in transaction messaging across its European network.
process_and_outcome: Santander received regulatory approval for its systems during ECB audits, maintained zero enforcement actions related to payment execution, and reported enhanced efficiency in annual compliance filings.

example_name: MoneyGram EU Cross-Border Remittance Compliance
example_background: MoneyGram International enhanced its compliance for euro-denominated cross-border remittances in EU states following PSD2 updates. The company integrated automated screening for all incoming and outgoing payments in euros and national currencies of opted-in states, ensuring adherence to execution speeds and fee disclosures across its agent network.
process_and_outcome: Passed multiple regulatory examinations by Central Bank of Ireland and ECB, achieving full compliance certification and avoiding penalties while expanding services.

example_name: European Commission Enforcement Against Unfair SEPA Direct Debit Practices
example_background: In 2018, the European Commission identified practices among certain payment service providers that involved unauthorized alterations to direct debit mandates for SEPA transactions in euro. These practices occurred in cross-border euro-denominated direct debit schemes where payee PSPs processed refunds or modifications without proper payer authorization, leading to disputes over transaction validity. The issue arose in the context of the Single Euro Payments Area (SEPA) framework, which standardizes euro payments across EU member states, affecting millions of consumers and businesses relying on direct debits for recurring payments such as utilities and subscriptions. The practices involved insufficient verification of mandate details, resulting in erroneous debits from payer accounts between PSPs.
process_and_outcome: The Commission conducted an investigation under competition rules, leading to commitments from involved PSPs to revise their procedures for handling SEPA direct debit mandates and refunds. No fines were imposed, but the PSPs agreed to enhanced compliance measures, including better mandate authentication processes, with monitoring by the Commission until implementation.
source_rule: 4.   Articles 6, 7 and 8 lay down rules regarding direct debit transactions denominated in euro between the payment service providers of the payee and of the payer.
involved_parties: Payment Service Providers in SEPA zone (payee and payer PSPs)
relevant_rules: Articles 6, 7, 8 of SEPA Direct Debit Regulation

example_name: Banco Santander SEPA Direct Debit Mandate Verification Failure
example_background: Banco Santander, operating in Spain and other EU countries, faced issues in 2017-2019 with its SEPA Core Direct Debit scheme where payer accounts were debited without fully validated mandates. This occurred in transactions denominated in euro between Santander (as payee PSP in some cases) and various payer PSPs across the SEPA area. The bank processed direct debits for clients like telecom providers, but lapses in electronic mandate checks led to invalid transactions, prompting consumer complaints and refunds. The background involved high-volume automated processing systems that did not adequately cross-check mandate details as required for inter-PSP direct debit flows.
process_and_outcome: The Bank of Spain investigated following consumer reports, resulting in a €10.3 million fine in 2020 for deficiencies in SEPA direct debit handling, including non-compliance with mandate rules. Santander implemented system upgrades and trained staff on SEPA procedures.
source_rule: 4.   Articles 6, 7 and 8 lay down rules regarding direct debit transactions denominated in euro between the payment service providers of the payee and of the payer.

example_name: National Bank of Belgium Fine on KBC Bank for SEPA Direct Debit Errors
example_background: KBC Bank, a major Belgian PSP, was involved in incidents from 2016-2018 where it failed to properly execute euro-denominated SEPA direct debit transactions as a payer PSP. Transactions initiated by payee PSPs were rejected or delayed due to inadequate processing of mandate references and creditor identifiers, affecting inter-PSP transfers for salaries, rents, and services. The context was the rollout of SEPA Direct Debit Core, where KBC's systems did not fully align with rules governing the exchange of direct debit instructions between PSPs, leading to operational disruptions and payer complaints.
process_and_outcome: The National Bank of Belgium (FSMA) conducted an inspection, imposing a €250,000 fine in 2019. KBC was required to audit its SEPA systems and report remediation steps, which included software updates for compliant mandate handling.

example_name: Deutsche Bank PSD2 SEPA Direct Debit Compliance Audit Success
example_background: Deutsche Bank in Germany underwent a 2020 regulatory audit of its euro-denominated SEPA direct debit operations, covering transactions between its PSP role and other EU PSPs. The bank demonstrated full implementation of mandate authentication, refund processing within 8 working days, and accurate creditor-borrower identifiers as per SEPA rules. This was part of broader PSD2 compliance efforts, handling high volumes of cross-border direct debits for corporate clients without reported disputes, showcasing effective inter-PSP coordination.
process_and_outcome: BaFin audit confirmed full compliance, with no findings or penalties. The bank received recognition for best practices in SEPA implementation, continuing operations without restrictions.

example_name: ING Group Netherlands SEPA B2B Direct Debit Penalty
example_background: ING in the Netherlands processed SEPA Business-to-Business (B2B) direct debits in euro from 2015-2017, where as payer PSP, it did not enforce the 'no refund' rule under Article 8 for B2B schemes, allowing unauthorized refunds. This involved transactions between ING and payee PSPs for business payments, disrupting the irrevocable nature intended for B2B direct debits. The issue stemmed from uniform processing systems not distinguishing Core from B2B schemes properly.
process_and_outcome: De Nederlandsche Bank fined ING €1.1 million in 2018 after investigation. ING updated its systems to segregate scheme handling and compensated affected parties.

example_background: During the 2014 SEPA migration deadline, BNP Paribas across France and other EU countries successfully transitioned all euro direct debit transactions to SEPA formats. As both payee and payer PSP, the bank ensured compliance with inter-PSP rules for mandate storage, execution, and refunds, handling over 100 million transactions annually without migration-related failures. This involved extensive testing of XML formats and creditor identifiers.
process_and_outcome: French ACPR regulator audit post-migration confirmed 100% compliance, with BNP Paribas cited as a model for SEPA adoption, avoiding any enforcement actions.

example_background: In 2019, BBVA encountered fraudulent SEPA direct debit mandates in euro transactions as payer PSP, where payee PSPs submitted invalid creditor details. example_name: Westpac Banking Corporation PSD2 Payee Verification Failures
example_background: Westpac, one of Australia's largest banks, processed over 23 million international fund transfers involving children under suspicious circumstances between 2013 and 2019. The transactions involved remittances to the Philippines where the ultimate recipients, or payees, were not subjected to adequate due diligence or monitoring as required under payment services frameworks. The bank's automated systems did not flag or report these transactions, which were linked to potential child exploitation activities. This occurred despite regulatory requirements for financial institutions to identify and verify the intended recipients of funds in cross-border payments to prevent misuse. The issue came to light during a routine compliance audit that revealed systemic deficiencies in the bank's payment processing infrastructure, particularly in handling international wires where payee information was incomplete or unverified. Westpac's failure to maintain records of payee identities contributed to prolonged undetected flows of funds.
process_and_outcome: AUSTRAC conducted an investigation starting in 2019, leading to civil penalty proceedings in the Federal Court of Australia. In 2020, Westpac entered into an enforceable undertaking and agreed to pay a A$1.3 billion penalty, the largest in Australian corporate history at the time. The bank was required to overhaul its transaction monitoring systems, appoint an independent reviewer, and implement enhanced payee verification protocols. No criminal charges were filed against individuals.
source_rule: 4. ‘payee’ means a natural or legal person who is the intended recipient of funds which have been the subject of a payment transaction;
relevant_rules: Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006 (Australia)
Payment Services Directive 2 (PSD2) principles on payee identification
Chapter 47 of the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Rules

example_name: HSBC Switzerland Suspicious Payment Processing
example_background: HSBC Private Bank (Suisse) SA handled over 480 million euros in payments between 2000 and 2015 linked to tax evasion schemes involving clients from emerging markets. Payments were executed to numerous payees, including trusts and nominees, without verifying their status as intended recipients or screening for politically exposed persons. The bank's systems relied on self-reported payee information without independent corroboration, leading to undetected flows to high-risk jurisdictions. Audits later revealed that payee identities were not documented in compliance with payment regulation standards, contributing to prolonged exposure.
process_and_outcome: Swiss regulator FINMA issued a public statement in 2020, ordering HSBC Switzerland to strengthen controls and pay CHF 30 million in profit disgorgement. No further fines were imposed, but the bank invested in remediation including AI-based payee screening.

example_name: Wirecard AG Accounting Scandal
example_background: Wirecard AG, a German payment processing firm, operated as a provider of payment services across Europe and Asia, handling payment accounts for merchants and facilitating payment orders. The company expanded rapidly by offering third-party acquiring services where it held payment accounts on behalf of clients and processed transactions. Investigations revealed that Wirecard misrepresented its financial position by fabricating €1.9 billion in cash balances supposedly held in Asian escrow accounts linked to payment processing activities. These fictitious assets were tied to payment services operations, including the management of payer accounts and execution of payment orders. The scandal emerged when auditors could not verify the existence of these funds, leading to questions about the integrity of Wirecard's payment processing infrastructure and client fund handling. The company's business model relied heavily on being a payment services provider under EU frameworks, serving numerous payers through its accounts.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and EY conducted audits, the Frankfurt public prosecutor's office launched a criminal investigation, Wirecard filed for insolvency in June 2020 with debts over €3.5 billion, CEO Markus Braun was arrested on charges of false accounting and market manipulation, and the company was delisted from the stock exchange. Regulators imposed bans on short-selling and later enhanced oversight on payment firms.
source_rule: 3. ‘payer’ means a natural or legal person who holds a payment account and allows a payment order from that payment account, or, where there is no payment account, a natural or legal person who gives a payment order;

example_name: MoneyGram International AML Violations
example_background: MoneyGram, a US-based global money transfer company operating in the EU, provided payment services allowing individuals to send funds via agents without traditional payment accounts, fitting the definition of payers giving payment orders directly. Between 2008 and 2012, the company processed over 170,000 suspicious transactions worth around €78 million through its EU and international network, often involving structured small transfers to obscure origins. These transactions were linked to criminal networks using MoneyGram's services for fund transfers where senders acted as payers issuing orders without holding accounts with the firm. Internal compliance programs failed to detect patterns such as rapid fund recycling and use of fake IDs at agent locations across Europe and beyond. The operations involved numerous payers initiating transfers that regulators later identified as part of larger laundering schemes.
process_and_outcome: US Department of Justice and FinCEN investigated, leading to a €18 million civil penalty in 2012 and a deferred prosecution agreement requiring enhanced compliance measures. MoneyGram implemented new anti-fraud technology and agent monitoring, with ongoing oversight until 2017.
source_rule: 3. ‘payer’ means a natural or legal person who holds a payment account and allows a payment order from that payment account, or, where there is no payment account, a natural or legal person who gives a payment order;

example_name: Santander Consumer Finance Unauthorized Payments
example_background: Santander Consumer Finance, part of the Santander Group operating in Spain and other EU countries, provided consumer credit and payment services. In 2019, the entity continued deducting insurance premiums via direct debit from customer payment accounts even after policy cancellations, affecting thousands of payers who held accounts and authorized payment orders for premiums. Customers reported that despite notifying Santander of policy terminations with insurers like DKV Seguros, the deductions persisted for months, with the bank processing orders from payers' accounts without updated authorizations. This occurred in the context of bundled credit and insurance products where Santander acted as the payment services provider executing orders on behalf of payers.
process_and_outcome: Banco de España investigated following consumer complaints, imposed a €10.3 million fine in 2023 for unauthorized direct debit transactions and inadequate customer information, ordered remediation including refunds to affected customers.

example_name: Wirecard PSP Licensing Failure
example_background: Wirecard AG, a German payment service provider and fintech company, operated across Europe offering payment processing, card issuing, and acquiring services. The company expanded rapidly, becoming one of the largest PSPs in Europe, handling billions in transactions annually. It was listed on the DAX index and provided services to major clients including international retailers and online platforms. However, investigations revealed that Wirecard lacked proper authorization for certain payment services in multiple jurisdictions, including operations that fell under the scope of Directive 2007/64/EC without obtaining necessary licenses. This occurred amidst broader accounting irregularities where €1.9 billion in assets were reported as existing in Asian escrow accounts but could not be verified, leading to questions about the legitimacy of its entire business model as a PSP. Wirecard had positioned itself as a compliant entity by claiming adherence to PSD requirements, but regulators found discrepancies in its licensing status for cross-border payment services, particularly in the UK and Ireland where subsidiaries operated without full authorization.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and the European Central Bank initiated probes in 2019, escalating to insolvency in June 2020. The company filed for bankruptcy with debts over €3.5 billion. German prosecutors charged executives including CEO Markus Braun with fraud. The ECB withdrew Wirecard's banking license, and it was fined by Irish regulators for unauthorized PSP activities. BaFin faced criticism for oversight failures.

example_name: N26 Unauthorized PSP Expansion
example_background: N26, a German neobank and payment service provider, obtained a full banking license from BaFin in 2016 but sought to expand payment services across the EU. Operating as a mobile banking app with IBAN accounts and card issuing, N26 launched additional payment initiation and processing features in countries like France and Spain without securing specific PSP authorizations under PSD2 in those jurisdictions. The company processed millions of transactions monthly, partnering with Mastercard for card services. Regulators noted that N26's cross-border passporting relied on its German banking license, but certain account information and payment initiation services required separate notifications or licenses, which were not fully completed. This came to light during routine supervisory checks amid N26's rapid user growth to over 5 million customers.
process_and_outcome: BaFin issued a public warning in 2020 and imposed growth restrictions, limiting new customer onboarding in Germany to 50,000 per month. The ECB and national authorities required N26 to apply for full PSD2 licenses in target markets. N26 complied by submitting applications and faced temporary service suspensions in some countries, with no monetary fines but ongoing enhanced supervision.

example_name: PayPal PSD Licensing Enforcement
example_background: PayPal (Europe) Services Ltd, based in Luxembourg, has operated as a licensed payment institution under PSD since 2007, providing money remittance, payment processing, and e-money services across the EU. In 2018, the Central Bank of Ireland conducted a thematic review of PSPs and identified that PayPal's subsidiaries in certain member states were conducting limited payment services without proper individual authorizations or passporting notifications as required under Article 26 of PSD1. This involved small-scale acquiring activities for merchants. PayPal's global scale, handling over 10 billion transactions yearly, amplified the scrutiny. The firm maintained compliance programs but overlooked notification deadlines for new services in peripheral markets like Bulgaria and Romania.
process_and_outcome: The Central Bank of Ireland fined PayPal €1.6 million in 2020 for breaches including failure to notify passporting. PayPal remedied by submitting notifications, enhancing internal compliance monitoring, and cooperating fully without admitting liability. No service disruptions occurred.

example_name: Worldline PSD2 Charge Transparency Fine
example_background: Worldline, a major European payment service provider, processed payment transactions and levied charges on users without providing clear pre-transaction information about the amounts or conditions of those charges. This occurred across multiple member states where the company operated payment initiation and account information services under PSD2. Customers were not adequately informed about the direct and indirect fees linked to transactions, leading to complaints about unexpected deductions from accounts. The issue spanned several years and involved high-volume transaction processing for merchants and consumers alike.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Central Bank (DNB) conducted an investigation following consumer reports and market supervision activities. In 2023, Worldline was fined €250,000 for non-compliance with charge disclosure requirements. The company was required to review and update its systems to ensure future transparency.
source_rule: 9. ‘charge’ means a charge levied by a payment service provider on the payment service user and directly or indirectly linked to a payment transaction;

example_name: Adyen Unauthorized Charge Practices
example_background: Adyen, a Dutch payment service provider, faced scrutiny for its handling of charges in payment transactions. Between 2018 and 2021, the company applied certain fees to transactions without explicit user consent or clear disclosure in advance, particularly for cross-border payments where currency conversion charges were indirectly embedded. This affected numerous merchant clients and end-users who experienced discrepancies between advertised and actual costs.
process_and_outcome: The Authority for Consumers and Markets (ACM) investigated after receiving complaints. In 2022, Adyen received a warning and was ordered to cease the practices and compensate affected parties up to €5 million in potential claims. No direct fine was imposed but remedial actions were mandated.
source_rule: 9. ‘charge’ means a charge levied by a payment service provider on the payment service user and directly or indirectly linked to a payment transaction;

example_name: Santander Consumer Finance Charge Violation
example_background: Santander Consumer Finance in the Netherlands levied charges on payment transactions related to consumer credit without properly separating them from other fees or providing itemized breakdowns. This involved installment payment services where transaction-linked charges were not distinctly identified, affecting thousands of customers over a period from 2019 to 2022. The lack of transparency led to user confusion regarding the exact costs associated with each payment.
process_and_outcome: DNB investigated based on supervisory reviews and imposed a €1.86 million penalty in 2023. Santander was directed to enhance its disclosure processes and reimburse overcharged customers.
source_rule: 9. ‘charge’ means a charge levied by a payment service provider on the payment service user and directly or indirectly linked to a payment transaction;

example_name: ClearBank PSD2 Compliance Achievement
example_background: ClearBank, a UK-based payment service provider, implemented robust systems for handling charges in payment transactions compliant with PSD2. From the regulation's implementation in 2018, the bank provided real-time pre-transaction notifications of all charges, including breakdowns for direct fees and indirect costs like currency conversion, ensuring users received complete information before authorizing payments. This approach was applied across its API services for third-party providers.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) reviewed ClearBank's operations during authorization and ongoing supervision, confirming full adherence with no findings of deficiency. The bank received authorization as a PSD2-compliant entity and expanded services without enforcement actions.
source_rule: 9. ‘charge’ means a charge levied by a payment service provider on the payment service user and directly or indirectly linked to a payment transaction;

example_name: Bunq Transparent Fee Structure
example_background: Bunq, a Dutch neobank and payment service provider, designed its app to display all charges linked to payment transactions upfront. Since PSD2 rollout, users see exact amounts for transfers, withdrawals, and conversions before confirmation, with no hidden indirect fees. This includes dynamic notifications for real-time payments and international transactions, serving millions of users across Europe.
process_and_outcome: DNB supervision confirmed compliance in multiple audits from 2019-2023, with Bunq highlighted as a model for charge transparency. No penalties issued, and the bank maintained its license seamlessly.
source_rule: 9. ‘charge’ means a charge levied by a payment service provider on the payment service user and directly or indirectly linked to a payment transaction;

example_name: N26 PSD2 Charge Handling
example_background: N26, a German mobile bank, integrated PSD2-compliant charge disclosures into its platform. For every payment transaction, users receive notifications specifying the charge amount, whether direct or indirect, linked to services like SEPA transfers and card payments. This was rolled out in 2019 and includes features for TPP integrations with full fee transparency.
process_and_outcome: BaFin conducted assessments and authorized N26's PSD2 services without issues. Regular reporting showed consistent adherence, avoiding any supervisory interventions.
source_rule: 9. ‘charge’ means a charge levied by a payment service provider on the payment service user and directly or indirectly linked to a payment transaction;

example_name: Mollie Payment Charges Enforcement
example_background: Mollie, a European payment processor, applied charges to merchants for transaction processing without clear separation or pre-approval disclosures between 2017 and 2020. This included indirect fees bundled into processing rates for online payments, leading to disputes from small businesses unaware of the full costs linked to each transaction.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch ACM investigated following merchant complaints and issued a €575,000 fine in 2021. Mollie updated its contracts and fee structures as remediation.
source_rule: 9. ‘charge’ means a charge levied by a payment service provider on the payment service user and directly or indirectly linked to a payment transaction;

example_name: Revolut PSD2 Fee Transparency
example_background: Revolut, a UK-licensed e-money institution, enhanced its PSD2 compliance by introducing mandatory charge previews for all payment transactions. Users see breakdowns of fees, including FX markups as indirect charges, prior to execution. Implemented in 2018, this covers transfers, card spends, and API access for partners.
process_and_outcome: FCA supervision verified compliance during 2020-2023 reviews, with no enforcement actions. Revolut expanded PSD2 offerings successfully.
source_rule: 9. ‘charge’ means a charge levied by a payment service provider on the payment service user and directly or indirectly linked to a payment transaction;

example_name: PayPal EU Charge Disclosure Fine
example_background: PayPal Europe Services handled payment transactions where charges were not consistently disclosed pre-transaction, particularly for cross-border and currency conversion services from 2016-2019. Users encountered unexpected fees post-execution, linked directly to the transactions processed through the platform.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg's CSSF regulator investigated and imposed a €9 million fine in 2020. PayPal committed to system overhauls and customer refunds.
source_rule: 9. ‘charge’ means a charge levied by a payment service provider on the payment service user and directly or indirectly linked to a payment transaction;

example_name: Deutsche Bank London AML Failures
example_background: Deutsche Bank AG in London facilitated over $2.1 billion in payments between Russia and various European and Asian correspondent banks from 2012 to 2017. The payments stemmed from mirror trades where Russian clients instructed their PSP to execute trades mirrored by Deutsche Bank's PSP role. The bank failed to apply customer due diligence on these payment instructions, processed suspicious transactions without query, and maintained inadequate records of payer details in violation of PSP obligations for payment orders.
process_and_outcome: UK Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) fined Deutsche Bank £163 million in 2021. The US Office of the Comptroller of the Currency also imposed a $425 million penalty. Deutsche Bank enhanced its payments compliance framework and conducted a historical review.
source_rule: 8. ‘payment order’ means an instruction by a payer or payee to his payment service provider requesting the execution of a payment transaction;

example_name: Sberbank PSD2 Non-Compliance Fine
example_background: Sberbank, Russia's largest bank, was investigated for shortcomings in implementing strong customer authentication (SCA) for electronic payment orders under PSD2. From 2019 to 2021, the bank processed payment transactions where payers instructed remote electronic payments without fully applying SCA exemptions or frictionless flows, leading to improper execution of payment instructions. Issues included inadequate risk assessments for payee-initiated transactions and failures to log payer instructions accurately.
process_and_outcome: The Bank of Russia imposed a 100 million rubles fine in 2021. Sberbank upgraded its authentication systems and aligned processes with PSD2 requirements for payment service providers.
source_rule: 8. ‘payment order’ means an instruction by a payer or payee to his payment service provider requesting the execution of a payment transaction;
relevant_rules: Article 4(9) PSD2 definition of payment order

example_name: HSBC Mirror Trading Scheme
example_background: HSBC Europe processed approximately $11.5 billion in payment orders linked to a Russian mirror trading scheme from 2011 to 2014. Russian clients instructed their Moscow broker to execute share purchases, with corresponding sales and payment instructions routed through HSBC's London PSP branch. HSBC failed to conduct due diligence on these cross-border payment instructions, question suspicious patterns, or maintain records of ordering parties, despite internal alerts on the transactions.
process_and_outcome: FCA imposed a £64.4 million fine in 2021. US DOJ deferred prosecution with $99 million payment. HSBC improved its payment order screening and correspondent banking controls.
source_rule: 8. ‘payment order’ means an instruction by a payer or payee to his payment service provider requesting the execution of a payment transaction;

example_name: Revolut PSD2 Authorization and User Protection
example_background: Revolut, a UK-based fintech offering mobile banking and payment services, applied for and obtained an EU electronic money institution license in Lithuania in 2018 to serve as a payment service provider under PSD2. The company provides services to millions of payment service users across the EEA, enabling transfers, currency exchange, and account information services for payers and payees. Revolut implemented strong customer authentication (SCA) requirements, open banking APIs for third-party providers, and segregated client funds to protect users. During the COVID-19 pandemic, it handled surges in transactions while maintaining incident reporting and user redress mechanisms, demonstrating operational resilience. This setup ensures that users, whether sending or receiving payments, have access to secure services compliant with EU standards.
process_and_outcome: The Bank of Lithuania granted the license after rigorous assessment. Revolut passed multiple supervisory reviews, including SCA implementation audits in 2019-2020. No major enforcement actions were taken; instead, it received approval for expansion and was listed as a compliant PSP in ECB registries.
source_rule: 6. ‘payment service user’ means a natural or legal person making use of a payment service in the capacity of either payer or payee, or both;
relevant_rules: Regulatory Technical Standards on Strong Customer Authentication (RTS SCA)

example_name: N26 PSD2 Licensing Revocation Threat
example_background: N26, a German mobile bank, received a full banking license from BaFin in 2016 and expanded payment services to users across Europe, acting as both payer and payee facilitator through its app. In 2021, BaFin identified failures in anti-money laundering (AML) monitoring and transaction screening for payment service users, particularly in handling high-risk transactions involving natural persons as payers. N26 had onboarded over 2 million users without adequate automated checks, leading to suspicious activity reports exceeding thresholds. This impacted the protection and proper servicing of payment users under PSD2, as delays in reporting affected user fund safeguards.
process_and_outcome: BaFin conducted inspections in 2021, imposing a €4.25 million fine and capping new customer onboarding at 50,000 pending remediation. N26 invested €100 million in compliance upgrades and lifted the cap after demonstrating improvements in 2022.
source_rule: 6. ‘payment service user’ means a natural or legal person making use of a payment service in the capacity of either payer or payee, or both;

example_name: PayPal Europe PSD2 User Fund Safeguarding
example_background: PayPal (Europe) SARL, licensed in Luxembourg, operates as an e-money institution providing payment services to EU users as payers and payees. It holds user balances in segregated accounts and offers buyer/seller protection programs. In its operations, PayPal manages billions in transactions annually, ensuring funds are ring-fenced from company assets to protect users in case of insolvency, directly aligning with payment service user protections.
process_and_outcome: The Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CSSF) conducted routine audits confirming safeguarding compliance. PayPal maintained its license through renewals and was praised in EBA reports for best practices in user fund protection.
source_rule: 6. ‘payment service user’ means a natural or legal person making use of a payment service in the capacity of either payer or payee, or both;

example_name: Monzo PSD2 Open Banking Implementation
example_background: Monzo, a UK digital bank with EU passporting, launched PSD2-compliant APIs in 2018, allowing third-party providers to access user payment accounts with consent. Serving users as payers for PISP services and payees for AISP data aggregation, Monzo handled millions of API calls while enforcing SCA for all initiations, enhancing user control over payment services.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) approved Monzo's PSD2 compliance framework. It achieved 100% SCA adoption ahead of deadlines and participated in EBA innovation hubs without issues.
source_rule: 6. ‘payment service user’ means a natural or legal person making use of a payment service in the capacity of either payer or payee, or both;

example_name: Starling Bank PSD2 TPP Access Issues
example_background: Starling Bank, a UK challenger bank, faced challenges in 2019 when it initially restricted access for certain third-party providers (TPPs) seeking to service payment users via its APIs. Users acting as payers complained about denied payment initiation services, prompting regulatory scrutiny over contestation processes for payment service users under PSD2. Starling argued security concerns but was required to provide non-discriminatory access.
process_and_outcome: The FCA investigated following TPP complaints, mandating API improvements. Starling resolved issues by Q4 2019, implementing dedicated TPP portals and avoiding fines through voluntary remediation.
source_rule: 6. ‘payment service user’ means a natural or legal person making use of a payment service in the capacity of either payer or payee, or both;

example_name: Klarna PSD2 Licensing and User Services
example_background: Klarna, a Swedish buy-now-pay-later provider, obtained a payment institution license from Finansinspektionen in 2017, enabling it to offer payment services like direct bank transfers to EU users as payers. It processes deferred payments where users are payees from merchants, ensuring clear terms and dispute resolution for all parties involved in the payment chain.
process_and_outcome: Finansinspektionen renewed Klarna's license multiple times post-PSD2 implementation. The firm complied with SCA rollouts and user information duties, supporting expansion without enforcement.
source_rule: 6. ‘payment service user’ means a natural or legal person making use of a payment service in the capacity of either payer or payee, or both;

example_name: Worldline PSD2 Fine for User Authentication Failures
example_background: Worldline, a French payment services provider, was found deficient in implementing strong customer authentication for electronic payments involving users as payers in 2020. The company processed card transactions without full SCA compliance during the initial PSD2 deadline extensions, exposing users to fraud risks in payer-payee interactions.
process_and_outcome: The ACPR (French regulator) fined Worldline €300,000 in 2021 after audits revealed non-compliance rates. Worldline upgraded systems and reported full SCA adoption by 2022.
source_rule: 6. ‘payment service user’ means a natural or legal person making use of a payment service in the capacity of either payer or payee, or both;

example_name: Westpac PSD2 Payment Services Violations
example_background: Westpac Banking Corporation, one of Australia's largest banks, processed over 23 million international fund transfers involving children traveling from Australia to countries including the Philippines, Thailand, and Vietnam between 2013 and 2019. These transactions were not subjected to the required automated monitoring and reporting under customer identification and transaction monitoring obligations. The bank's systems failed to detect patterns of multiple low-value transfers that collectively amounted to significant sums, with daily transfer volumes reaching up to 1,400 on certain days. This occurred during a period when Westpac was implementing a new core banking system, leading to inadequate safeguards for payment transaction monitoring. The transfers were initiated by payers placing funds through the bank's international money transfer services, fitting the definition of payment transactions under payment services regulations.
process_and_outcome: AUSTRAC commenced civil penalty proceedings in November 2019. In 2020, Westpac entered into an enforceable undertaking and agreed to pay a A$1.3 billion penalty, the largest in Australian corporate history. The bank was required to appoint an independent expert to oversee remediation of its anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing program, including enhancements to payment transaction monitoring systems.
source_rule: 7. ‘payment transaction’ means an act, initiated by a payer or by or through a payee, of placing, transferring or withdrawing funds, irrespective of any underlying obligations between the payer and the payee;
relevant_rules: Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006 (Australia)

example_name: HSBC Mexico AML Payment Processing Failures
example_background: HSBC's Mexican subsidiary processed approximately USD 881.7 million in bulk cash shipments from Mexico to the US between 2007 and 2008 on behalf of suspected drug cartels. These involved wire transfers and physical cash deposits treated as payment transactions, with inadequate monitoring of structuring patterns where funds were broken into sub-USD 10,000 amounts to avoid reporting thresholds. HSBC's global payment systems failed to apply enhanced due diligence to high-risk cross-border payments, despite knowing the high-risk nature of Mexican clients.
process_and_outcome: In 2012, HSBC agreed to a US$1.9 billion deferred prosecution agreement with the US Department of Justice, including forfeiture of US$1.256 billion and a civil penalty. The bank overhauled its compliance program, including payment transaction monitoring tools.
source_rule: 7. ‘payment transaction’ means an act, initiated by a payer or by or through a payee, of placing, transferring or withdrawing funds, irrespective of any underlying obligations between the payer and the payee;
involved_parties: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC)

example_name: MoneyGram Consumer Fraud Payment Transfers
example_background: From 2008 to 2012, MoneyGram International allowed fraudulent actors to exploit its money transfer services, resulting in over USD 100 million in avoidable consumer losses. Fraudsters initiated payment transactions by directing victims to send funds via MoneyGram's wire transfer network for fake prizes, emergencies, or romance scams. The company's agents cashed out these transactions without verifying identities or stopping suspicious patterns, such as repeat transfers from the same senders to high-risk destinations.
process_and_outcome: In 2012, MoneyGram entered a USD 18 million civil money penalty settlement with the US Department of Justice and Federal Trade Commission. As part of a deferred prosecution agreement, it implemented a stringent corporate compliance program focused on transaction monitoring.
source_rule: 7. ‘payment transaction’ means an act, initiated by a payer or by or through a payee, of placing, transferring or withdrawing funds, irrespective of any underlying obligations between the payer and the payee;

example_name: BNY Mellon Foreign Exchange Hidden Payments
example_background: From 2008 to 2015, BNY Mellon charged over 100 clients undisclosed higher prices for foreign exchange transactions executed through its 'standing instruction' service, where clients authorized automatic conversions of incoming payments. The bank pocketed the difference between internal rates and client rates on trillions in payment transactions, without disclosure. These were payer-initiated transfers of funds converted upon receipt.
process_and_outcome: In 2015, BNY Mellon paid USD 714 million to settle SEC charges and a New York Department of Financial Services investigation. The bank refunded clients and improved pricing transparency in payment services.

relevant_rules: EU Markets in Financial Instruments Directive (MiFID II)

example_name: Ripple Labs XRP Cryptocurrency Sanctions Violations
example_background: Between 2013 and 2016, Ripple Labs Inc. sold over 5.5 billion XRP tokens, including to individuals and entities in sanctioned jurisdictions like the US Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC)-blocked addresses. These sales involved transfers of digital assets treated as payment transactions on the XRP Ledger, without implementing adequate geoblocking or sanctions screening on fund transfers initiated by payers.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the US Federal Trade Commission settled with Ripple for USD 4 million in civil penalties. Ripple enhanced its compliance program with transaction screening for payment-like transfers.

example_name: PayPal EU PSD2 Strong Customer Authentication Implementation
example_background: PayPal, as a licensed payment institution under PSD2, rolled out Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) across the European Economic Area starting in 2019. This involved applying multi-factor authentication for all electronic payment transactions initiated by payers or payees, including card payments, account-to-account transfers, and withdrawals. PayPal integrated biometric verification, device binding, and dynamic linking to secure fund placements and transfers, processing billions of transactions annually while maintaining service continuity.
process_and_outcome: PayPal achieved full compliance ahead of deadlines, with no major regulatory enforcement actions. The implementation reduced fraud rates and was recognized in industry reports for effective execution of PSD2 requirements.

example_name: Adyen PSD2 Compliance and Innovation
example_background: Adyen, a Dutch payment service provider, obtained an EU-wide PSD2 license in 2018 and implemented a comprehensive framework for payment transactions. This included access to account (XS2A) interfaces for third-party providers, secure APIs for initiating payer fund transfers, and real-time monitoring of placing, transferring, and withdrawing funds. Adyen handled high-volume e-commerce payments for clients like Uber and Spotify, ensuring all transactions met the broad definition under PSD2.
process_and_outcome: Adyen received regulatory approval without penalties and was praised in supervisory reviews for robust implementation. The firm expanded services under PSD2, demonstrating scalable compliance.

example_name: Skrill PSD2 Account Information Services
example_background: Skrill (Paysafe Group), a UK-based e-money institution, complied with PSD2 by authorizing regulated Account Information Service Providers (AISPs) and Payment Initiation Service Providers (PISPs) to access user payment accounts. This enabled secure initiation of fund transfers and withdrawals by payees or third parties, with Skrill providing dedicated APIs for transaction data aggregation and payment initiation across Europe, covering millions of cross-border payment transactions.
process_and_outcome: Skrill integrated PSD2 requirements seamlessly, passing FCA audits and avoiding enforcement. It positioned itself as a leader in open banking-compliant payment services.
