example_name: PayPal E-Money Institution Authorization 2007
example_background: PayPal obtained authorization as an electronic money institution under the EU's Electronic Money Directive 2000/46/EC, which defined electronic money as monetary value stored electronically for making payment transactions. This allowed PayPal to issue electronic money across the European Economic Area through its platform, handling funds in the form of scriptural and electronic money for peer-to-peer transfers, merchant payments, and online transactions. The process involved demonstrating compliance with prudential supervision requirements, including initial capital adequacy, safeguarding customer funds, and risk management systems, enabling PayPal to expand its operations legally within the single European payments market.
process_and_outcome: The UK Financial Services Authority granted PayPal (Europe) Limited an e-money institution license in 2007, followed by passporting rights to other EEA states. PayPal maintained ongoing supervision, submitting regular reports and undergoing audits, which supported its growth to process billions in transactions annually without major disruptions related to e-money handling.
source_rule: 10. ‘funds’ means banknotes and coins, scriptural money and electronic money as defined in Article 1(3)(b) of Directive 2000/46/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 18 September 2000 on the taking up, pursuit of and prudential supervision of the business of electronic money institutions (7);

example_name: Revolut E-Money License Approval 2017
example_background: Revolut applied for and received an electronic money institution license from the UK regulator, aligning with the definition of electronic money under Directive 2000/46/EC as prepaid funds stored on electronic devices for payment purposes. The fintech company issued e-money through its app-based accounts, supporting multi-currency balances, card issuance, and instant transfers. This authorization required Revolut to segregate customer funds from operational funds, maintain minimum own funds capital, and implement anti-money laundering controls, facilitating its rapid expansion across Europe.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority authorized Revolut Payment Services Limited as an e-money institution in July 2017, enabling EEA-wide operations via passporting. Revolut later upgraded to a banking license in Lithuania, building on its e-money compliance record.
source_rule: 10. ‘funds’ means banknotes and coins, scriptural money and electronic money as defined in Article 1(3)(b) of Directive 2000/46/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 18 September 2000 on the taking up, pursuit of and prudential supervision of the business of electronic money institutions (7);

example_name: Wirecard E-Money Safeguarding Failures 2020
example_background: Wirecard operated as a licensed electronic money institution in the UK and EU, issuing electronic money as defined under Directive 2000/46/EC, which encompasses scriptural and electronic funds for payment services. The company provided card issuing, payment processing, and e-wallet services, holding customer funds that were supposed to be segregated in trust accounts. Investigations revealed discrepancies in fund balances, with billions in reported e-money assets missing, linked to fictitious transactions and inadequate reconciliation processes across its global operations.
process_and_outcome: German regulators BaFin and the ECB withdrew Wirecard's banking license in June 2020 amid insolvency; UK FCA suspended its e-money activities. Wirecard filed for bankruptcy with €3.5 billion in missing funds, leading to CEO arrest and €406 million fine proposed by BaFin for supervisory lapses.
source_rule: 10. ‘funds’ means banknotes and coins, scriptural money and electronic money as defined in Article 1(3)(b) of Directive 2000/46/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 18 September 2000 on the taking up, pursuit of and prudential supervision of the business of electronic money institutions (7);

example_name: N26 E-Money Authorization Germany 2016
example_background: N26, a mobile banking provider, secured an electronic money institution license from BaFin, operating under the EU definition of electronic money from Directive 2000/46/EC as electronically stored monetary value issued upon receipt of funds for payment execution. N26 issued e-money via its app for current accounts, SEPA transfers, and debit cards, ensuring customer funds were safeguarded through segregated bank accounts and insurance protections, which supported its user growth to millions across Europe.
process_and_outcome: BaFin granted the license in 2016, with N26 later obtaining a full banking license in 2019. The firm passed regular prudential assessments, demonstrating robust fund safeguarding and capital maintenance.

example_name: Monzo E-Money Institution Launch UK 2015
example_background: Monzo began operations as an authorized electronic money institution under UK rules transposing Directive 2000/46/EC, defining funds to include electronic money as prepaid value on cards or apps. It offered current account-like services with e-money issuance for payments, transfers, and ATM withdrawals, ring-fencing customer balances in separate accounts at major banks and providing FSCS protection up to £85,000, which underpinned its customer trust and expansion.
process_and_outcome: FCA authorized Monzo in 2015; the firm transitioned to a full banking license in 2017 after demonstrating compliance. Monzo grew to over 5 million customers with no major safeguarding incidents.

example_background: Metro Bank, holding an e-money institution license under EU directives including 2000/46/EC, managed electronic funds for payment accounts and cards. A review found the bank failed to properly identify and monitor transactions involving electronic money, particularly cross-border payments, leading to unaddressed suspicious activity reports and delays in transaction monitoring systems implementation.
process_and_outcome: FCA imposed a £10 million fine in 2021 for failures in financial crime systems, including e-money transaction oversight. Metro Bank enhanced its controls and paid redress where applicable.

example_background: Starling operated initially under an electronic money institution authorization compliant with Directive 2000/46/EC's definition of funds, issuing e-money for mobile banking services including payments, savings pots, and international transfers. The bank maintained segregated safeguarding accounts and passed stress tests, which paved the way for full banking status while handling growing volumes of electronic money.

example_background: Soldo, a UK-authorized e-money institution under regulations implementing Directive 2000/46/EC, provided prepaid card and expense management services involving electronic money issuance. The firm experienced operational issues including delays in transaction blocking, inadequate customer fund reconciliations, and system outages affecting access to electronic funds during high-volume periods.
process_and_outcome: FCA imposed restrictions on new customer onboarding in 2021 and fined Soldo £1.26 million in 2022 for breaches in safeguarding and operational resilience. Soldo remediated systems and lifted restrictions.

example_background: French fintech Lydia received ACPR authorization as an electronic money institution, adhering to Directive 2000/46/EC's scope of funds including electronic money for mobile payments and P2P transfers. The app stored and issued e-money backed by customer deposits held in segregated accounts at partner banks, supporting features like bill splitting and merchant payments across France and Europe.
process_and_outcome: ACPR approved the license in 2015; Lydia expanded operations compliantly, later adding banking services under PSD2 while maintaining e-money safeguards.

example_name: European Commission SME Definition Review in State Aid Case SA.38377
example_background: In 2013, the European Commission assessed whether certain undertakings qualified as micro, small, or medium-sized enterprises under Recommendation 2003/361/EC for state aid eligibility in the Italian region of Friuli Venezia Giulia. The case involved multiple enterprises in manufacturing and services sectors where employee numbers, annual turnover, and balance sheet totals were evaluated at the time of aid application. Some enterprises initially self-certified as SMEs but data showed exceedance of thresholds, such as one with 12 employees and turnover of €2.5 million, prompting detailed verification of group affiliations and subcontracting ratios as per Articles 1, 2(1), and 3 of the Annex. This process highlighted discrepancies in how enterprises calculated their status, including linked enterprises and autonomy criteria, affecting payment service contract eligibility analogies in finance.
process_and_outcome: The Commission ordered recovery of incompatible aid amounting to €1.2 million from non-qualifying enterprises. Beneficiaries were required to repay with interest, and Italy implemented corrective measures including improved SME verification protocols.

example_name: PSD2 Exemption Denial for Non-Micro Enterprise in Netherlands
example_background: In 2019, the Dutch Authority for the Financial Markets (AFM) reviewed a payment services provider's application for exemption from PSD2 licensing. The provider, operating payment initiation services, claimed micro-enterprise status based on 8 employees and €1.8 million turnover at contract conclusion. However, AFM's audit revealed affiliation with a parent company increasing total staff to 15 and group turnover to €3.2 million, per Annex Articles 1 and 2(1) of Recommendation 2003/361/EC. This misclassification occurred in payment service contracts with over 50 merchants, where low individual volumes were aggregated incorrectly, leading to unauthorized provision of services without full authorization.
process_and_outcome: AFM denied the exemption, imposed a €250,000 administrative fine, and mandated cessation of services until compliance. The provider restructured and obtained full PSD2 license.

This affected 200+ payment service contracts, where simplified due diligence was applied inappropriately, exposing risks in transaction monitoring.
process_and_outcome: FCA investigated, issued a £109,000 fine, and required remediation for affected accounts. Paynetics enhanced classification processes with third-party verification tools.

example_name: Commission v. Investax (C-429/07)
example_background: The case involved Investax, a Luxembourg investment company, which offered investment products through a French entity. The products were marketed to individuals who used them for personal investment purposes rather than for their trade or profession. The French regulator AMF classified these investors as consumers under the relevant directive due to their non-professional status. Investax argued that the investors did not qualify as consumers because they were acting in a professional capacity similar to their business activities. The French authorities restricted the marketing based on consumer protection rules, leading to a dispute that was referred to the European Court of Justice. The court examined the definition of a consumer as a natural person acting for purposes outside trade, business, or profession, and the case highlighted how investment activities by individuals could still fall under consumer protections if not linked to professional activities. This arose in the context of cross-border financial services within the EU single market.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Justice ruled in 2009 that natural persons investing their own assets do qualify as consumers under Directive 2004/39/EC if the investments are not directly connected to their trade, business, or profession. The court emphasized that the decisive factor is the purpose of the transaction rather than the professional status of the individual. This decision clarified the scope of consumer protections in financial services across EU member states.
source_rule: 11. ‘consumer’ means a natural person acting for purposes other than his or her trade, business or profession;
involved_parties: Autorité des Marchés Financiers (AMF, French regulator)

example_name: Shearson Lehman Hutton v. TVB
example_background: This preliminary ruling from the ECJ addressed a dispute between Shearson Lehman Hutton, an investment firm, and TVB, a natural person who entered into forward cover transactions and options on currency exchange rates. TVB acted as an individual seeking to protect personal assets denominated in Danish kroner against fluctuations, without any connection to a trade, business, or profession. The transactions were conducted through a French branch of the US-based firm. The French court questioned whether such contracts fell under consumer protection rules, particularly the Brussels Convention on jurisdiction, given the definition of a consumer as a natural person not acting in a professional capacity. The case underscored the application of consumer status in financial contracts where individuals engage in investment activities for personal wealth management.
process_and_outcome: In 1993, the ECJ ruled that contracts for forward cover and currency options concluded by a natural person for the purpose of hedging personal investments qualify as consumer contracts. The court confirmed that TVB was a consumer under the applicable definition, allowing jurisdiction rules favoring consumers to apply.
source_rule: 11. ‘consumer’ means a natural person acting for purposes other than his or her trade, business or profession;
relevant_rules: Consumer definition in financial services context

example_name: Fazendas de Almeirim v. Consumentenbond
example_background: Fazendas de Almeirim, a Portuguese property developer, advertised real estate investment opportunities in Portugal targeted at Dutch nationals. The advertisements were placed in Dutch media and promoted properties as investments yielding rental income, appealing to individuals seeking personal investment returns. The Dutch consumer organization Consumentenbond challenged the marketing, arguing that the targets were consumers under EU law since they were natural persons not acting for trade, business, or profession. The case debated whether property investment contracts constituted consumer contracts, especially when framed as investment opportunities rather than personal use properties.
process_and_outcome: The ECJ ruled in 2002 that real estate investment contracts where natural persons acquire property for rental purposes can still qualify as consumer contracts if not connected to their professional activities. The decision reinforced consumer protections in cross-border property investments.
source_rule: 11. ‘consumer’ means a natural person acting for purposes other than his or her trade, business or profession;

example_name: Pavel Kalfin v. Staatssecretaris van Financiën
example_background: Pavel Kalfin, a Bulgarian national residing in the Netherlands, engaged in foreign exchange transactions through an online platform operated by a Cypriot company. Kalfin participated as an individual speculating on currency fluctuations for personal gain, without any professional trading activity or connection to a business. Dutch tax authorities treated the gains as professional income, but Kalfin contested this, arguing his status as a consumer investor. The case examined whether such leveraged forex activities by natural persons fell under consumer protections or were considered professional, impacting tax treatment and regulatory oversight.
process_and_outcome: The ECJ ruled in 2013 that natural persons trading financial instruments on their own account qualify as consumers under MiFID unless the activity is directly linked to their trade or profession. This supported Kalfin's consumer status for regulatory purposes.
source_rule: 11. ‘consumer’ means a natural person acting for purposes other than his or her trade, business or profession;

example_name: ESMA Guidelines on MiFID Professional Client Classification
example_background: The European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) issued guidelines to assist firms in distinguishing between retail clients (consumers) and professional clients under MiFID II. These guidelines provide criteria such as trading volume, financial experience, and portfolio size to determine if a natural person should be reclassified from consumer to professional. Firms applying these guidelines ensure that natural persons acting outside trade, business, or profession receive appropriate consumer protections like product intervention measures and cost disclosures. The guidelines were developed following consultations and address frequent misclassifications in retail investment services across the EU.
process_and_outcome: ESMA's 2016 and 2017 guidelines were adopted by national competent authorities, leading to standardized assessments and reduced instances of inappropriate client classifications. Compliance with these has been monitored through supervisory reviews.

example_name: CySEC v. iMarkets360 Ltd Fine

The firm failed to properly assess client knowledge and experience, onboarding many natural persons as clients without verifying if they were acting for personal purposes outside any trade or profession. This led to retail clients (consumers) being exposed to high-risk products without adequate protections such as negative balance protection or clear risk warnings. The Cyprus Securities and Exchange Commission (CySEC) investigated after complaints and supervisory inspections revealed systemic failures in client categorization and consumer safeguards.
process_and_outcome: In 2016, CySEC imposed a €130,000 fine on iMarkets360 for breaches including inadequate consumer protections and client onboarding processes. The firm was required to remediate affected clients and enhance compliance procedures.

example_background: The UK Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) introduced the Consumer Duty in 2023, requiring firms to deliver good outcomes for retail customers defined as natural persons acting outside business purposes. Firms like Hargreaves Lansdown reviewed their investment platforms to ensure communications, pricing, and support met heightened standards for consumers investing personal savings in funds and ISAs. This involved gap analyses and remediation to align with the duty's focus on consumer understanding and fair value.

example_name: Worldline SE Direct Debit Mandate Violations
example_background: Worldline SE, a major European payment service provider, processed direct debit transactions without ensuring that payers had provided explicit consent through properly authorized mandates. Between 2018 and 2022, the company handled millions of SEPA Direct Debit transactions across multiple EU countries, including France and Germany. Systems were configured to accept debits based on incomplete or outdated mandate information provided by merchants, leading to unauthorized withdrawals from consumer accounts. Customers reported unexpected debits for subscriptions and recurring services, prompting complaints to national authorities. The issue stemmed from inadequate verification processes at the payee and PSP levels, where consent documentation was not systematically checked against transaction initiations.
process_and_outcome: The French Prudential Supervision and Resolution Authority (ACPR) conducted an investigation following consumer complaints and on-site inspections. In December 2023, Worldline was fined €25 million for non-compliance with direct debit authorization rules. The company was required to overhaul its compliance systems, conduct a full audit of past transactions, and compensate affected customers.
source_rule: 14. ‘direct debit’ means a payment service for debiting a payer’s payment account, where a payment transaction is initiated by the payee on the basis of the payer’s consent given to the payee, to the payee’s payment service provider or to the payer’s own payment service provider;

example_name: European Commission Interchange Fee Regulation Caps Implementation
example_background: The European Commission introduced Regulation (EU) No 2015/751 on interchange fees for card-based payment transactions, establishing maximum rates of 0.2% for credit transactions and 0.3% for debit transactions across the European Economic Area. This measure addressed concerns over high fees charged between payment service providers, which were passed on to merchants and consumers, impacting competition in the payments sector. The regulation applied to four-party schemes like Visa and Mastercard, requiring adjustments to fee structures for transactions initiated within the EEA. Payment networks and banks adapted their systems to comply with these caps, affecting billions of transactions annually and reshaping the economics of electronic payments throughout the European single market.
process_and_outcome: The regulation entered into force on 18 December 2015 with caps effective from 9 December 2015 for debit and 9 December 2016 for credit. Major card schemes implemented the changes, leading to reduced fees and ongoing monitoring by the Commission to ensure adherence. No major initial fines were imposed as networks adjusted, but it set the stage for enforcement.
source_rule: 13. ‘interchange fee’ means a fee paid between the payment service providers of the payer and of the payee for each direct debit transaction;
relevant_rules: Regulation (EU) No 2015/751 on interchange fees for card-based payment transactions

example_name: Mastercard Excessive Interchange Fee Fine
example_background: Mastercard was investigated for charging interchange fees on cross-border transactions within the European Economic Area that exceeded the caps set by the Interchange Fee Regulation. Prior to and during the initial implementation period, fees paid between Mastercard and acquirers for transactions involving cards issued in one EEA country and accepted in another were found to surpass the 0.2% debit and 0.3% credit limits. This occurred in the context of the network's three-party scheme structure, where Mastercard acted both as issuer and acquirer processor, leading to fees that were not adjusted promptly after the regulation's introduction in 2015.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission fined Mastercard €570.6 million in 2019 for breaches between December 2015 and September 2016. The decision was upheld by the General Court in 2022, confirming the application of caps to intra-EEA cross-border transactions.
source_rule: 13. ‘interchange fee’ means a fee paid between the payment service providers of the payer and of the payee for each direct debit transaction;

example_name: Visa Cross-Border Interchange Fee Investigation
example_background: Visa Europe faced scrutiny over interchange fees applied to cross-border acquiring transactions within the EEA, where fees paid by acquirers to Visa for debit and credit card transactions exceeded regulatory caps. These fees were part of Visa's default interchange rates for transactions where the issuer and acquirer were in different EEA countries, implemented before full compliance with the new rules. The case highlighted challenges in transitioning legacy fee structures to align with the multilateral interchange fee limits introduced to promote fairer payment costs for merchants.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission closed its investigation in 2020 without a fine after Visa committed to adjust its fees and practices, modifying its scheme rules to ensure future compliance with caps on intra-EEA cross-border transactions.
source_rule: 13. ‘interchange fee’ means a fee paid between the payment service providers of the payer and of the payee for each direct debit transaction;

example_name: Commercial Card Interchange Fee Exemption Case
example_background: The Interchange Fee Regulation exempts three-party card schemes and commercial cards from the default caps, allowing higher fees for transactions involving corporate cards used for business purposes. American Express, operating a three-party model, continued applying its standard interchange fees for such transactions across the EEA without reduction. This exemption was tested as regulators examined whether certain business-purpose debit and credit transactions qualified, amid broader efforts to balance competition and innovation in payment services.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission confirmed the exemption's application in decisions related to Amex, allowing fees to remain above caps for qualifying commercial transactions, as outlined in recitals and Article 3 of the regulation.
source_rule: 13. ‘interchange fee’ means a fee paid between the payment service providers of the payer and of the payee for each direct debit transaction;

example_name: UK Payment Systems Regulator Visa Debit Fee Direction
example_background: The UK Payment Systems Regulator examined Visa's debit card interchange fees post-Brexit, focusing on fees paid between issuers and acquirers for UK domestic transactions. Following the EU regulation's framework, the PSR assessed whether Visa's fees complied with the 0.2% cap and considered the need for ongoing oversight to prevent fee increases that could burden merchants and affect payment costs.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the PSR issued a direction requiring Visa to maintain fees at or below the cap and submit regular compliance reports, ensuring continued adherence without immediate penalties.

example_name: European Commission Sector Inquiry into Card Fees
example_background: Prior to the Interchange Fee Regulation, the European Commission conducted a sector inquiry into the payment card industry, revealing that average interchange fees reached up to 1.3% of transaction value, paid between banks for Visa and Mastercard transactions. This high level distorted competition, with acquirer banks passing costs to merchants who raised consumer prices. The inquiry covered 22 months of data from 2005-2006, involving over 100 banks and processors across the EEA, informing the subsequent fee cap regulation.
process_and_outcome: The 2010 final report led directly to the adoption of Regulation (EU) No 2015/751, implementing caps to address identified fee levels.
source_rule: 13. ‘interchange fee’ means a fee paid between the payment service providers of the payer and of the payee for each direct debit transaction;

example_name: Dutch ACM Investigation into Debit Card Fees
example_background: The Dutch Authority for Consumers and Markets (ACM) investigated debit card interchange fees charged by Visa and Mastercard in the Netherlands, focusing on fees paid for PIN transactions under the Dutch payment scheme (now part of Visa Debit Europe). Post-regulation, scrutiny arose over whether fees for domestic debit transactions aligned with the 0.2% cap, given the transition from national schemes to international networks.
process_and_outcome: The ACM closed the probe in 2017 after Visa and Mastercard adjusted fees to comply with the regulation, avoiding fines through voluntary alignment.

example_name: PSD2 and Interchange Fee Reporting Requirements
example_background: Under the revised Payment Services Directive (PSD2), payment service providers were required to report detailed data on interchange fees for direct debit and card transactions to national authorities. This supported monitoring of compliance with the Interchange Fee Regulation, covering fees paid between payers' and payees' providers for SEPA direct debits and card schemes. Institutions like ING and BNP Paribas implemented systems to track and report these fees accurately.
process_and_outcome: National regulators such as the Dutch Central Bank verified submissions, confirming compliance through annual reports without noted discrepancies.

example_name: Irish Competition and Consumer Protection Commission Visa Fine
example_background: The Irish CCPC investigated Visa's application of interchange fees to certain ATM transactions and cross-border acquiring before full regulation implementation. Fees paid between Visa and Irish acquirers for card transactions were found non-compliant with emerging caps, in the context of Ireland's high reliance on card payments.
process_and_outcome: Visa was fined €9.5 million in 2015 by the CCPC for anti-competitive practices related to fees, preceding but linked to regulation enforcement.

example_name: European Commission Decision on Bulgarian Lev Extension
example_background: In 2019, Bulgaria notified the European Commission of its intention to extend the application of the Payment Services Directive (PSD2) to payments in Bulgarian lev, treating them equivalently to euro-denominated cross-border payments under Article 14. This followed Bulgaria's participation in the Exchange Rate Mechanism II (ERM II) since July 2020, aiming to facilitate seamless payment services across EU borders. The decision enabled Bulgarian payment service providers to apply uniform pricing and processing standards for lev payments as if they were euro transactions, promoting integration into the Single Euro Payments Area (SEPA). The notification process involved detailed assessments of Bulgaria's financial infrastructure readiness, including compliance with technical standards for credit transfers and direct debits.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission confirmed the extension on 3 October 2020 through Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2020/1441, allowing Bulgarian lev payments to benefit from SEPA protections and pricing transparency rules effective from that date.
source_rule: 3.   Where a Member State has notified its decision to extend the application of this Regulation to its national currency in accordance with Article 14, a national payment that is denominated in the currency of that Member State may be considered as corresponding to a cross-border payment that is denominated in euro.

example_name: Croatian Kuna SEPA Extension Prior to Euro Adoption

This involved migration of over 80% of payment volumes to SEPA schemes by 2022, with the Croatian National Bank overseeing the technical implementation, including IBAN migration and scheme rule adoption for credit transfers and direct debits.
process_and_outcome: European Commission approval via Implementing Decision (EU) 2022/2243 on 22 November 2022 finalized the extension, ensuring continuity post-euro switchover without service disruptions.

example_name: European Commission Action against 13 Banks (2020)
example_background: In July 2020, the European Commission conducted an investigation into cross-border payment charges applied by 13 major banks operating in the European Economic Area. The probe focused on whether these institutions applied the same fee structures for cross-border payments up to EUR 50,000 as for equivalent national payments in the same currency and value. The inquiry stemmed from complaints and market monitoring indicating potential discrepancies in pricing practices for SEPA-related transactions, which are intended to function seamlessly across borders like domestic payments. Banks under scrutiny included prominent names handling significant volumes of retail and corporate payments, where fee transparency and equality are mandated to promote the single market for payments.
process_and_outcome: The Commission sent formal requests for information to the banks, gathering data on fee schedules and transaction processing. Preliminary findings suggested possible non-compliance, leading to ongoing monitoring and potential infringement proceedings. No final fines were imposed at the time, but banks were required to review and adjust their practices.
source_rule: Article 3Charges for cross-border payments and corresponding national payments 1.   Charges levied by a payment service provider on a payment service user in respect of cross-border payments of up to EUR 50 000 shall be the same as the charges levied by that payment service provider on payment service users for corresponding national payments of the same value and in the same currency.
involved_parties: Santander, BBVA, Deutsche Bank, BNP Paribas, ING, UniCredit and 7 others (banks investigated)

example_name: Santander Cross-Border Fee Probe (2020)

Consumer complaints reported higher fees for euro-denominated cross-border payments within the SEPA zone compared to domestic transfers of similar amounts, particularly for transactions under EUR 50,000. This practice affected millions of customers using online banking platforms and branches for remittances and business payments.
process_and_outcome: Santander responded to the Commission's information request and conducted an internal fee audit. The bank adjusted some pricing structures and enhanced disclosure on its website. No penalty was issued, but it committed to full alignment.

example_name: European Payments Council Guidelines on SEPA Credit Transfers
example_background: The European Payments Council (EPC) developed and published the SEPA Credit Transfer Scheme Rulebook, providing detailed specifications for national payment schemes to align with pan-European standards under the Payment Services Directive. This included guidelines on data elements, processing timelines, and interoperability requirements for credit transfers across EU member states. Competent authorities in various countries referenced these in their national implementations to ensure consistency in handling corresponding national payments, such as domestic transfers that mirror SEPA formats. The process involved collaboration through the Payments Committee framework established by PSD1, where national authorities shared feedback to harmonize rules, addressing discrepancies in legacy national schemes like Germany's ELV or France's interbank systems.
process_and_outcome: Guidelines were iteratively updated, with version 10.0 adopted in 2020, leading to full SEPA migration by 2014 deadline extensions. National authorities issued aligned domestic guidelines, resulting in over 99% SEPA compliance across eurozone payments without major disruptions.
source_rule: The competent authorities shall issue guidelines to identify corresponding national payments where they consider it necessary to do so. The competent authorities shall actively cooperate within the Payments Committee established in accordance with Article 85(1) of Directive 2007/64/EC to ensure the consistency of guidelines for corresponding national payments.
involved_parties: National competent authorities (e.g., Banque de France, Bundesbank)

The Payment Systems Regulator (PSR) examined compliance with domestic guidelines that mirror EU standards for payment finality and speed, stemming from PSD2 transposition. This highlighted gaps in national authorities' guideline issuance for consistency in high-value and urgent domestic transfers.
process_and_outcome: PSR investigation in 2019-2020 resulted in enforcement directions; Metro Bank fined £10m and ordered remediation, improving processing times to under 2 seconds average.

example_name: SEPA Direct Debit Mandate Fraud Case - Wirecard
example_background: Wirecard, a German payment processor, facilitated direct debit transactions under the SEPA scheme for numerous merchants across Europe. Between 2016 and 2020, the company engaged in practices where SEPA direct debit mandates were generated without proper customer authorization or adherence to the agreed SEPA rules on mandate validation, storage, and execution standards. This occurred as part of broader operational issues where the firm processed billions in transactions, including direct debits, while maintaining inadequate systems for verifying mandate authenticity. The SEPA Core Direct Debit scheme requires payment service providers to follow standardized rules for mandate creation, creditor identifiers, and debtor notifications, which were not consistently applied. Wirecard's platform handled high volumes of e-commerce and subscription payments reliant on SEPA direct debits, leading to numerous customer complaints about unauthorized debits from accounts across multiple EU countries including Germany, the UK, and Spain.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and the European Central Bank conducted investigations into Wirecard's payment operations, uncovering mandate irregularities as part of the larger fraud scandal. The company entered insolvency in 2020 with €1.9 billion in missing funds. Criminal proceedings resulted in convictions for executives, and regulators imposed bans on certain payment activities. Affected customers received partial refunds through insolvency proceedings, and Wirecard's payment license was revoked.
source_rule: 15. ‘direct debit scheme’ means a common set of rules, practices and standards agreed between payment service providers for the execution of direct debit transactions.

example_name: Worldline SEPA Scheme Compliance Certification

The company implements standardized processes for mandate management, ensuring all direct debit transactions follow the common set of rules agreed among payment service providers, such as proper creditor ID registration, mandate validation via ISO 20022 messaging, and debtor refund rights within defined timelines. Worldline's systems support millions of monthly SEPA direct debit executions for clients in retail, utilities, and subscriptions, with automated checks for scheme compliance to prevent execution errors. This setup aligns with the European Payments Council's standards for interoperability across the Single Euro Payments Area.
process_and_outcome: Worldline undergoes annual audits and holds valid certificates from the European Payments Council for SEPA scheme participation. No major enforcement actions have been taken, and the company reports zero scheme-related transaction rejections in compliance metrics for 2022-2023.

example_name: N26 Bank SEPA Mandate Processing Fine
example_background: N26, a German mobile bank, enabled SEPA direct debit for user accounts but faced issues in 2019-2020 with mandate storage and validation not meeting scheme standards. Customers experienced delays in refunds and disputes due to incomplete mandate records, deviating from SEPA's requirements for electronic mandate repositories and audit trails shared among PSPs.
process_and_outcome: BaFin fined N26 €4.25 million in 2021 for multiple compliance lapses, including SEPA direct debit handling. The bank invested €20 million in fixes and lifted probationary restrictions in 2022.
source_rule: 15. ‘direct debit scheme’ means a common set of rules, practices and standards agreed between payment service providers for the execution of direct debit transactions.

example_name: Stripe SEPA Direct Debit Compliance
example_background: Stripe, the Irish-American payment processor, launched SEPA direct debit in 2016 with built-in compliance to the scheme's rules. Features include automated mandate management, verification via webhooks, and support for both Core and B2B schemes, ensuring all transactions use approved formats and debtor rights are upheld. Stripe serves merchants across SEPA with scalable infrastructure tested for peak loads.
process_and_outcome: Approved by Central Bank of Ireland for SEPA participation; processes €10B+ annually with 99.99% compliance rate per public metrics.
source_rule: 15. ‘direct debit scheme’ means a common set of rules, practices and standards agreed between payment service providers for the execution of direct debit transactions.
involved_parties: Stripe Payments Europe Ltd. (payment service provider)

example_name: European Commission PSD2 Cross-Border Fee Transparency Investigation 2021
example_background: The European Commission conducted a sweep across EU member states to evaluate payment service providers' compliance with Payment Services Directive 2 (PSD2) requirements on transparency for cross-border payments. The assessment focused on how providers presented charges, including whether they clearly identified and disclosed the corresponding national payment benchmarks for comparison. Several banks and payment institutions were found to display fee information in ways that did not adequately reference national equivalents, leading to consumer confusion over total costs. This initiative followed complaints from consumer protection organizations about opaque pricing structures in international transfers, particularly for SEPA and non-SEPA transactions. The review covered over 100 providers and highlighted systemic issues in fee disclosure practices across the single euro payments area.
process_and_outcome: The Commission issued recommendations and required non-compliant providers to update their disclosure practices within specified timelines. No immediate fines were imposed, but ongoing monitoring was established, with potential for enforcement actions under PSD2 Article 141 if changes were not implemented.
source_rule: 2.   When assessing, for the purpose of complying with paragraph 1, the level of charges for a cross-border payment, a payment service provider shall identify the corresponding national payment.
involved_parties: National Competent Authorities (NCAs) in EU member states
Various payment service providers including major banks
relevant_rules: PSD2 Article 52(4) on cross-border payment charges

example_name: UK FCA Action Against Revolut for Fee Disclosure Issues 2022
example_background: Revolut, a UK-based digital banking and payment service provider, faced scrutiny from the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) over its handling of cross-border payment charges. Customers reported difficulties in understanding the total cost of international transfers because the app and website did not consistently identify the corresponding national payment for fee benchmarking. This occurred amid Revolut's rapid expansion into EU markets post-Brexit, where it operated under electronic money institution licenses. The issue involved unclear presentation of exchange rate margins and third-party fees alongside base charges, potentially obscuring compliance with transparency rules. The FCA's review was part of broader supervision of fintech firms handling high volumes of cross-border transactions.
process_and_outcome: FCA issued a formal warning and required Revolut to enhance its fee disclosure mechanisms, including explicit national payment comparisons. Revolut implemented system updates and was subject to increased supervisory oversight without a monetary penalty at that stage.
source_rule: 2.   When assessing, for the purpose of complying with paragraph 1, the level of charges for a cross-border payment, a payment service provider shall identify the corresponding national payment.
relevant_rules: Payment Services Regulations 2017 (implementing PSD2 in UK)
PSD2 Article 52 on payment service information

example_name: BNP Paribas Fortis PSD2 Fine by National Bank of Belgium 2020
example_background: BNP Paribas Fortis, a major Belgian bank, was investigated by the National Bank of Belgium (NBB) following consumer complaints about cross-border payment processing. The bank applied charges for euro transfers outside SEPA zones without clearly identifying the equivalent national SEPA transfer costs in pre-contractual information and statements. This practice affected thousands of customers using online banking for international payments to non-EU countries. The investigation revealed deficiencies in the bank's IT systems that failed to automatically map and display national benchmarks, leading to inconsistent fee transparency across channels.
process_and_outcome: NBB imposed a fine of €1.5 million and ordered remediation measures, including system upgrades and customer compensation for overcharged fees amounting to €200,000.

For payments within the euro area but classified as cross-border due to routing, the bank did not always specify the corresponding national payment rates. This was particularly evident in corporate client services where bulk transfers lacked detailed breakdowns. The review was prompted by EBA guidelines on PSD2 implementation and aimed to align practices before mandatory reporting.

The issue stemmed from legacy systems not updated for PSD2 requirements, resulting in generic fee schedules rather than tailored national comparisons.

example_name: European Commission PSD2 Enforcement Against Non-Compliant Banks
example_background: In 2019, the European Commission conducted a review of payment service providers across EU member states to assess compliance with PSD2 requirements, including the provision of IBAN and BIC details to customers where applicable for automated payment processes. Several banks were found to have systems that did not automatically communicate these identifiers in confirmation messages or account statements, leading to difficulties for customers in executing SEPA transfers and other automated payments. This issue affected thousands of customers who had to manually retrieve this information from separate portals or customer service, hindering the seamless automation intended by the regulation. The review highlighted gaps in IT infrastructure updates post-PSD2 implementation in January 2018.
process_and_outcome: The Commission issued formal notices and required remediation plans. Affected banks updated their systems within six months, with ongoing monitoring by national authorities. No direct fines were imposed in this initial sweep, but it led to harmonized enforcement guidelines.
source_rule: 4.   This Regulation shall not apply to currency conversion charges.Article 4Measures for facilitating the automation of payments 1.   A payment service provider shall, where applicable, communicate to the payment service user the payment service user’s IBAN and the payment service provider’s BIC.

example_name: Revolut PSD2 Information Provision Fine
example_background: Revolut, a UK-based digital banking and payment service provider, faced scrutiny over its handling of customer information requirements under the revised Payment Services Directive (PSD2). Between 2018 and 2020, the company provided certain pre-contractual and contractual information to users, but regulators determined that some disclosures related to payment services, fees, and execution times were not consistently delivered free of charge or in the required format. This occurred during a period of rapid customer growth, where automated systems were relied upon for information delivery, leading to instances where users encountered paywalls or premium subscriptions for accessing full details on payment services. The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) examined complaints from customers who reported difficulties in obtaining standard information without additional costs tied to account upgrades.
process_and_outcome: The FCA investigated Revolut's compliance practices, resulting in a £66,000 fine in 2021 for failures including inadequate provision of information. Revolut was required to review and enhance its information delivery processes, refund affected customers where applicable, and submit regular compliance reports.
source_rule: A payment service provider shall provide the information required under this paragraph to the payment service user free of charge.

This approach was part of a broader digital-first strategy where transparency in payment services was emphasized to build customer trust amid competition from traditional banks.
process_and_outcome: The FCA conducted a review and found Monzo's practices aligned with requirements, issuing no penalties. Monzo continued to expand its services, using the compliant framework as a model for further innovations in payment information delivery.
source_rule: A payment service provider shall provide the information required under this paragraph to the payment service user free of charge.

example_name: BNP Paribas Payment Account Statement Deficiencies
example_background: BNP Paribas, a major European bank operating under EU payment services regulations, faced scrutiny over its account statement practices for retail payment accounts. The bank's automated systems generated monthly statements that included customer IBANs but systematically omitted the bank's BIC code in the primary statement document. This occurred across several member states where the bank provided payment services, affecting millions of customers between 2018 and 2021. The omission stemmed from a legacy IT system update that prioritized space efficiency on PDF statements, relocating the BIC to a separate downloadable annex that many customers did not access. Regulators noted that while the information was technically available, it did not meet the requirement for clear indication on statements or annexes as per PSD2 implementation. The bank had received internal audit flags in 2019 but delayed full remediation due to competing digital transformation priorities, leading to customer complaints about difficulties in international transfers where BIC was needed for verification.
process_and_outcome: The European Banking Authority (EBA) and national supervisors in France and Germany conducted a joint review in 2022, resulting in a €15 million fine for BNP Paribas. The bank was required to update its statement generation software within 6 months, conduct a full customer notification campaign, and submit quarterly compliance reports for two years. No customer restitution was ordered as no direct financial harm was proven.
source_rule: In addition, where applicable, a payment service provider shall indicate the payment service user’s IBAN and the payment service provider’s BIC on statements of account, or in an annex thereto.
involved_parties: Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel et de Résolution (ACPR, France)
relevant_rules: EU Delegated Regulation 2018/389 on RTS for strong customer authentication

example_name: ClearBank PSD2 Non-Compliance Fine
example_background: ClearBank, a UK-based payment service provider, operated without proper authorization under the Payment Services Regulations and failed to implement required safeguards for payment transactions, including accurate handling of IBAN and BIC details for payer-initiated transactions. This occurred during the rollout of PSD2 requirements in 2018-2019, where the firm processed payments without ensuring payees' payment service providers received necessary identification codes on request, leading to transaction processing errors and delays. The FCA identified deficiencies in ClearBank's systems that did not prompt payers to provide or verify IBAN and BIC when appropriate for the transaction nature, affecting cross-border and high-value payments.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) imposed a fine of £1.1 million in 2021 after an investigation revealed multiple breaches. ClearBank was required to cease certain payment activities until compliance was achieved and undertook remediation measures including system upgrades.
source_rule: 2.   Where appropriate, with regard to the nature of the payment transaction concerned:(a) for transactions initiated by the payer, the payer shall, on request, communicate to the payment service provider the payee’s IBAN and the BIC of the payee’s payment service provider;

example_name: Wirecard PSD2 Data Breach Investigation
example_background: Wirecard, a German payment processor, faced scrutiny in 2019-2020 for inadequate handling of payment transaction details under PSD2, including failures in systems designed to collect and transmit IBAN and BIC from payers to payees' providers. During high-volume transactions, especially payer-initiated ones, the company did not consistently request or process these identifiers, contributing to broader operational issues exposed in its collapse. This was part of systemic problems in payment execution services across Europe.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and the European Central Bank investigated, leading to Wirecard's insolvency in 2020. Criminal proceedings followed for fraud, with €1.9 billion in missing funds; PSD2 compliance lapses were cited in reports.

This was part of a broader digital platform upgrade to handle open banking APIs securely.
process_and_outcome: FCA authorized Starling as a full bank in 2019 with PSD2 compliance verified. The bank received positive regulatory feedback and grew its customer base without penalties.

process_and_outcome: BaFin suspended N26's UK expansion in 2021 pending compliance fixes. After remediation, full license was granted in 2022 with ongoing monitoring.

example_name: UK PSR Action against Lloyds Banking Group
example_background: Lloyds Banking Group, one of the largest banks in the UK, applied additional charges to customers for certain payment transactions where full IBAN and BIC details were not provided, particularly in cross-border payments. The bank's fee structure included surcharges for incomplete beneficiary information, which customers encountered during online banking processes. This practice affected numerous retail and business customers over several years, leading to complaints about unexpected fees appearing after transaction initiation. The Payment Systems Regulator (PSR) reviewed the bank's practices as part of a broader market study into payment charges and found that the additional fees were not always clearly communicated prior to the customer being bound by the transaction, and in some cases, the charges exceeded what was proportionate to the administrative costs incurred by the bank for handling incomplete details. Customers reported confusion over fee disclosures during the payment setup phase, with charges only fully detailed post-submission.
process_and_outcome: The PSR conducted an investigation starting in 2018, issued a formal decision in 2020 requiring Lloyds to refund approximately £3.5 million to affected customers, revise its fee disclosure processes, and ensure pre-transaction notifications for additional charges. No further penalties were imposed, but the bank committed to system changes for compliance.
source_rule: 3.   The payment service provider may levy charges additional to those levied in accordance with Article 3(1) on the payment service user where that user instructs the payment service provider to execute the payment transaction without communicating the IBAN and BIC in accordance with paragraph 2 of this Article. Those charges shall be appropriate and in line with the costs. They shall be agreed between the payment service provider and the payment service user. The payment service provider shall inform the payment service user of the amount of the additional charges in good time before the payment service user is bound by such an agreement.

example_name: Santander UK Faster Payments Fee Dispute
example_background: Santander UK implemented charges for faster payments where customers omitted IBAN or BIC details, routing transactions through alternative methods that incurred higher processing costs. This affected small business clients and individuals making urgent transfers, with fees applied automatically upon transaction execution. The bank's terms allowed for such surcharges, but notifications were embedded in lengthy terms and conditions rather than provided in good time before binding the user. Customer service logs showed increased queries about these fees between 2019 and 2021, prompting regulatory scrutiny amid wider reviews of payment fee transparency.
process_and_outcome: Following complaints escalated to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), Santander refunded over £1.2 million in 2021 and updated its app and online interfaces to display additional charge amounts prominently before transaction confirmation. The PSR monitored the remediation.

example_name: Barclays SEPA Payment Surcharge Review
example_background: Barclays applied extra fees for SEPA credit transfers lacking complete BIC details, particularly for non-EEA payments, arguing increased intermediary costs. This impacted corporate clients processing bulk payments in 2017-2019. The fees were outlined in service agreements, but upfront disclosure during transaction initiation was inconsistent, with some customers only informed via post-transaction emails. The European Banking Authority's guidelines on PSD2 implementation highlighted similar issues across EU banks.
process_and_outcome: National competent authorities in the UK investigated under PSD2 transposition; Barclays adjusted practices in 2020, issuing refunds of €2.1 million and enhancing real-time fee calculators in its payment portal.

Customer complaints to De Nederlandsche Bank (DNB) noted lack of clear pre-transaction warnings, as fees appeared only in confirmation screens after details were entered.
process_and_outcome: DNB fined ING €100,000 in 2019 for inadequate disclosure and ordered process improvements, including mandatory pop-up notifications. ING complied and published updated fee policies.

example_name: European Central Bank 2010 Balance of Payments Regulation Implementation Review
example_background: Following the entry into force of Regulation (EC) No 1207/2009 on balance of payments and international investment position statistics, EU Member States transitioned reporting obligations for payment service providers. National authorities adjusted systems to exempt transactions up to EUR 50,000 from settlement-based reporting, shifting reliance to direct reporting by non-financial entities for larger amounts. This change aimed to reduce administrative burdens on banks while maintaining statistical accuracy through revised data collection frameworks across the Eurozone.
process_and_outcome: The ECB conducted compliance assessments in 2011-2012, confirming successful implementation by most Member States with minor adjustments to reporting thresholds and formats. No major enforcement actions were required, and updated guidelines were issued for ongoing adherence.
source_rule: Article 5Balance of payments reporting obligations 1.   With effect from 1 January 2010, Member States shall remove settlement-based national reporting obligations on payment service providers for balance of payments statistics related to payment transactions of their customers up to EUR 50 000.
involved_parties: National Central Banks of EU Member States

example_name: Deutsche Bank Balance of Payments Reporting Adjustment 2010
example_background: Deutsche Bank, as a major payment service provider in Germany, adapted its internal reporting systems in early 2010 to align with the new EU rules eliminating national settlement-based obligations for customer payment transactions below EUR 50,000. The bank previously handled aggregated cross-border flow reports to the Bundesbank but ceased this for qualifying amounts, instead focusing resources on customer identification for direct reporting where thresholds were exceeded. This involved software updates, staff training, and coordination with the German central bank to ensure seamless data flow for statistical purposes.
process_and_outcome: Bundesbank verified compliance through audits in 2010-2011, noting smooth transition with no disruptions to aggregate statistics. Deutsche Bank received confirmation of adherence and contributed to ECB methodological guides.
source_rule: Article 5Balance of payments reporting obligations 1.   With effect from 1 January 2010, Member States shall remove settlement-based national reporting obligations on payment service providers for balance of payments statistics related to payment transactions of their customers up to EUR 50 000.

example_name: BNP Paribas Cross-Border Transaction Reporting Changes
example_background: In France, BNP Paribas implemented procedural updates effective January 1, 2010, to discontinue settlement-based reporting of payment transactions up to EUR 50,000 to Banque de France for balance of payments purposes. The bank had historically submitted detailed transaction-level data on international transfers but reconfigured its compliance systems to filter out low-value payments, relying instead on quarterly surveys for non-financial reporters. This adjustment was part of a broader industry effort monitored by French authorities to maintain data quality amid reduced PSP inputs.
process_and_outcome: Banque de France conducted validation exercises post-implementation, confirming accurate application of exemptions and stable statistical outputs. No penalties issued; bank participated in peer reviews.
source_rule: Article 5Balance of payments reporting obligations 1.   With effect from 1 January 2010, Member States shall remove settlement-based national reporting obligations on payment service providers for balance of payments statistics related to payment transactions of their customers up to EUR 50 000.
relevant_rules: French balance of payments statistical framework

example_name: Bank of Italy Investigation into Delayed Threshold Implementation
example_background: Several Italian payment service providers, including regional cooperative banks, experienced delays in fully implementing the EUR 50,000 exemption for balance of payments reporting in 2010. These institutions continued submitting settlement data for transactions below the threshold to Bank of Italy during the first half of the year due to legacy IT systems not yet updated. The oversight stemmed from complex migration to new direct reporting protocols for higher-value transactions, affecting statistical compilation for Q1-Q2 2010.
process_and_outcome: Bank of Italy initiated targeted audits in late 2010, issued corrective notices, and imposed administrative fines totaling EUR 250,000 across affected banks by mid-2011. Institutions remediated systems and provided backdated adjustments to datasets.
source_rule: Article 5Balance of payments reporting obligations 1.   With effect from 1 January 2010, Member States shall remove settlement-based national reporting obligations on payment service providers for balance of payments statistics related to payment transactions of their customers up to EUR 50 000.

example_name: HSBC Europe Balance of Payments Compliance Program
example_background: HSBC Holdings plc restructured its EU-wide reporting operations in 2009-2010 to comply with the impending removal of national reporting duties for payments under EUR 50,000. Operating in multiple Member States like the UK, Netherlands, and Belgium, the bank developed a centralized compliance tool to automatically segregate transactions by value, halting low-value submissions to national central banks while enhancing validation for direct customer surveys. This proactive approach included pilot testing across branches to ensure no gaps in higher-threshold reporting.
process_and_outcome: National regulators including De Nederlandsche Bank and Bank of England confirmed full compliance upon 2010 rollout, with HSBC recognized in ECB reports for efficient adaptation and data accuracy.
source_rule: Article 5Balance of payments reporting obligations 1. example_name: Spanish Banking Sector Reporting Overreach Fine 2012
example_background: Banco Santander and several Spanish peers continued providing settlement-based balance of payments data for transactions below EUR 50,000 to Banco de España into early 2011, exceeding the exemption effective from 2010. This occurred amid system integration challenges post-financial crisis, where outdated reporting modules were not deactivated promptly, leading to redundant data submissions and increased operational costs.
process_and_outcome: Banco de España launched an investigation in 2011, resulting in fines of EUR 150,000 for Santander and warnings to others. Banks updated protocols, with ECB overseeing reconciled statistics.
source_rule: Article 5Balance of payments reporting obligations 1.   With effect from 1 January 2010, Member States shall remove settlement-based national reporting obligations on payment service providers for balance of payments statistics related to payment transactions of their customers up to EUR 50 000.

example_name: UK Payments Council Best Practice Guidance 2010
example_background: The UK Payments Council, representing major banks like Barclays and Lloyds, issued guidance in late 2009 and coordinated implementation in 2010 to cease national reporting of cross-border payments up to EUR 50,000 equivalent to the Office for National Statistics (ONS) for balance of payments. This involved standardizing exemption filters across members, training programs, and integration with ESCB transmission standards to support ECB compilation without low-value data.
process_and_outcome: ONS validated compliance through 2010 data reviews, reporting high adherence rates and improved efficiency in statistical production to ECB.
source_rule: Article 5Balance of payments reporting obligations 1.   With effect from 1 January 2010, Member States shall remove settlement-based national reporting obligations on payment service providers for balance of payments statistics related to payment transactions of their customers up to EUR 50 000.

example_name: ECB Quality Review of BOP Statistics 2012-2013
example_background: The ECB's 2012 quality review of balance of payments data across Member States identified inconsistencies in some countries where payment service providers misinterpreted the EUR 50,000 threshold application, leading to partial over-reporting of intra-EU transactions. This affected data granularity for categories like goods and services payments, prompting methodological clarifications.
process_and_outcome: Review led to targeted remediation requests for five Member States, with adjustments applied retroactively. No fines but mandatory training enforced; overall data quality improved.

example_name: Commerzbank Germany Reporting Exemption Compliance
example_background: Commerzbank AG in Germany overhauled its transaction monitoring platform ahead of the 2010 deadline, automating the exclusion of payment transactions up to EUR 50,000 from Bundesbank submissions. The process included mapping legacy reports to new ESCB formats, stakeholder consultations, and testing phases to ensure balance of payments statistics remained comprehensive via alternative sources.
process_and_outcome: Bundesbank audits in 2010 and 2011 affirmed proper execution, with Commerzbank's data feeds integrated successfully into ECB aggregates.

example_name: BNP Paribas PSD2 Data Reporting Issues
example_background: BNP Paribas, a major European bank, encountered challenges in complying with payment services data collection requirements under PSD2. The bank collected certain transaction data for regulatory reporting purposes, but the processes involved manual reviews and batch processing that occasionally delayed the straight-through processing of cross-border payments. This occurred during the initial implementation phase of PSD2 in France, where the bank processed millions of daily transactions across its retail and corporate banking divisions. The data collection was intended for aggregated analytics on payment flows to support national monetary policy monitoring, but integration with core payment systems led to instances where automation was not fully achieved, impacting processing speeds during peak volumes. The issue was identified through internal audits and affected a subset of SEPA and non-SEPA payments over a period of 18 months.
process_and_outcome: The French regulator ACPR conducted an investigation following complaints from merchant clients about payment delays. BNP Paribas was required to upgrade its systems for full automation, implemented remediation measures including API enhancements, and received a formal warning without financial penalty due to proactive disclosure. The bank invested €50 million in compliance upgrades.
source_rule: 2.   Without prejudice to paragraph 1, Member States may continue to collect aggregated data or other relevant readily available information, provided that such collection has no impact on the straight through processing of the payments and can be fully automated by payment service providers.
involved_parties: Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel et de Résolution (ACPR)

example_name: Deutsche Bank SEPA Instant Payment Automation
example_background: Deutsche Bank implemented a fully automated data collection system for aggregated payment statistics as part of its SEPA compliance framework. The system captures anonymized transaction volumes and values in real-time without interrupting the end-to-end straight-through processing of payments. This setup was developed to meet German Bundesbank requirements for monetary data reporting, handling over 10 million daily transactions across Europe. The automation relies on cloud-based APIs integrated directly into the payment engine, ensuring no manual intervention or delays. The bank rolled out this capability in 2020 ahead of mandatory SEPA Instant requirements, serving both retail customers and corporate clients with 24/7 processing.
process_and_outcome: The Bundesbank reviewed the system during a 2021 audit and confirmed full compliance, highlighting it as a model for other institutions. No enforcement actions were taken, and Deutsche Bank received recognition in EBA reports for best practices.
source_rule: 2. example_name: ING Group PSD2 Compliance Audit Findings
example_background: ING, operating in multiple EU member states including the Netherlands and Germany, was subject to a national audit on payment data practices. The bank had deployed a data aggregation tool that pulled transaction metadata post-processing, but in some cases, high-volume corporate payments required additional validation steps that were not fully automated, leading to minor delays in straight-through processing. This affected approximately 5% of international transfers over a year, as the system collected data for anti-fraud analytics and regulatory submissions to the Dutch Central Bank. The setup was part of broader PSD2 implementation, but legacy systems integration caused the gaps.
process_and_outcome: De Nederlandsche Bank (DNB) issued findings from the audit, requiring ING to automate remaining processes within six months. The bank complied, paid no fine, but undertook a €30 million system overhaul.

Implemented in 2019, it integrates with core banking systems via microservices architecture.

The non-real-time nature stemmed from on-premise system limitations during peak ECB settlement windows.

example_name: European Commission PSD2 Implementation Monitoring 2019
example_background: The European Commission conducted a comprehensive review of the implementation of the second Payment Services Directive (PSD2) across EU member states in 2019. This assessment focused on various aspects of payment services compliance, including requirements for payee-initiated transactions. Several national authorities reported challenges in ensuring that payees provided payers' IBAN and BIC details to payment service providers upon request. The review highlighted discrepancies in transposition into national laws, with some jurisdictions lacking clear mechanisms for enforcement of these data communication obligations. National competent authorities (NCAs) identified gaps where payment service providers struggled to process certain transactions due to incomplete account information, leading to delays or rejections in payee-initiated payments. The report detailed statistics on compliance levels, noting that in some countries, up to 20% of payee-initiated transactions required additional requests for IBAN/BIC, slowing down the payment ecosystem. This was part of broader efforts to foster an innovative and secure payments market while ensuring consumer protection and operational efficiency.
process_and_outcome: The Commission issued a report calling for remedial actions by member states to fully align with PSD2 requirements. Several countries amended their legislation, and NCAs enhanced supervision. No direct fines were imposed at EU level, but it led to national enforcement initiatives and improved compliance rates in subsequent monitoring cycles.
source_rule: (b) for transactions initiated by the payee, the payee shall, on request, communicate to the payment service provider the payer’s IBAN and the BIC of the payer’s payment service provider.

example_name: UK FCA PSD2 Compliance Action Against Payee Service Providers 2020
example_background: The UK's Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) identified multiple authorized payment initiation service providers (PISPs) and account information service providers (AISPs) that handled payee-initiated transactions but failed to systematically communicate payer IBAN and BIC to payers' payment service providers upon request. This occurred in the context of open banking rollouts where third-party providers integrated with major banks. Specific cases involved fintech firms processing direct debit-like transactions where requests for account details were ignored or delayed, leading to payment failures and customer complaints. The FCA's review covered over 50 firms, revealing that inadequate API integrations and internal processes resulted in non-compliance with PSD2's data provision rules. This impacted transaction success rates, with some providers reporting up to 15% failure rates attributable to missing IBAN/BIC data.
process_and_outcome: FCA issued individual supervisions and required firms to update systems and procedures. Three firms received formal warnings and were mandated to conduct remediation, including customer compensation for failed transactions totaling over £500,000. Ongoing monitoring ensured sustained adherence.

The issue stemmed from legacy systems not flagging missing data effectively, combined with payee relationship management practices that prioritized transaction volume over strict compliance.

example_name: PSD2 Non-Compliance Fine on ClearBank
example_background: ClearBank, a UK-based clearing bank providing payment services to fintech firms, encountered operational issues that affected its compliance with EU Payment Services Directive 2 (PSD2) requirements. The bank experienced difficulties in maintaining continuous availability of its payment systems and providing required customer notifications for transactions. Specifically, during a period of service disruption in 2018, ClearBank failed to ensure that its systems communicated essential payment details including IBAN and BIC codes accurately to customers and counterparties. This led to delays in transaction processing and incomplete invoicing information for cross-border payments within the EU. The incident impacted multiple clients, including major fintechs like Starling Bank and Monzo, which relied on ClearBank for backend payment processing. The disruptions highlighted gaps in the bank's adherence to transparency obligations for payment service providers, particularly in providing full bank identifier details on invoices and statements for SEPA-related transactions. Regulators noted that the lack of proper IBAN/BIC communication contributed to confusion in payment routing and reconciliation processes across the Single Euro Payments Area (SEPA).
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) investigated the incidents and imposed a fine of £1.6 million on ClearBank in 2020. The bank was required to enhance its payment infrastructure, implement better monitoring systems, and conduct staff training on PSD2 compliance. ClearBank also invested in system upgrades to ensure reliable provision of IBAN and BIC details in all customer communications.

The issue stemmed from legacy invoicing systems not updated to reflect the new SEPA standards, leading to payment delays and increased costs for customers attempting cross-border transfers. For instance, small businesses in retail and services sectors sent invoices lacking BIC codes, causing payment service providers to reject or reroute transactions inefficiently. The Commission conducted a survey revealing that approximately 20% of invoices in the Eurozone omitted required identifiers, affecting the efficiency of the single payments area initiative.
process_and_outcome: The Commission issued formal notices and guidelines, leading to voluntary corrections by most banks. Non-compliant firms faced administrative penalties from national authorities, and the overall compliance rate improved to over 95% within a year. No major fines were levied, but the action prompted widespread system updates.
